When you wait with someone rather than for them — when you sit with a person who is suffering, or frightened, or in the slow endurance of something hard — you're doing something that doesn't have an efficient description. You're providing presence, which sounds modest but is one of the more difficult and important things one person can do for another. There is no technique for it. You just wait alongside. You let the time be what it is. You resist the urge to fix or fill or accelerate.

The hardest waiting of all might be the waiting that accompanies grief. After a death, particularly a sudden one, there can be a long period in which the bereaved person is waiting without quite knowing what they're waiting for. Not for the person to come back — they know better than that — but for the world to feel real again. For the loss to settle into a shape that the rest of life can organize itself around. This waiting has no reliable timeline. It can't be rushed. The people around the grieving person often become very uncomfortable with this waiting and try to do something about it, which is understandable, because the helplessness of waiting with someone is very uncomfortable. But usually the most helpful thing is the presence itself. The willingness to wait alongside.

---

I want to end with something smaller, because I've been getting fairly serious and I think the most true things about waiting are ordinary things.

There's a specific pleasure in certain kinds of waiting that I don't think gets acknowledged enough. Waiting for food when you're genuinely hungry. Waiting for a film to start when you're already in the seat with your coat off and you've given over the next two hours to someone else's story. Waiting for a friend you haven't seen in a long time, watching the arrivals door at an airport, every opening of it a small suspension and then a release.

These waitings are pleasurable because of their structure. They have a known end point, a promised reward, and the waiting itself becomes a kind of savoring. Anticipation is a form of experience, not just a prelude to it. The smell of something cooking isn't just information that food is coming — it's its own thing, its own enjoyment, and a good cook knows that making people wait a little is part of the meal.

I remember being a child and loving the specific quality of Christmas Eve over Christmas Day. The lights were the same, the decorations were the same, the food was in some ways better on the day itself. But Christmas Eve had something the day didn't. The waiting was so present you could feel its texture, almost see it in the air. Everything was about to happen. Nothing had happened yet. There was a particular silence in that particular waiting, and I remember it more clearly than I remember most Christmas Days.

I think this is what I've been circling around. The waiting is not the absence of experience. It is a specific kind of experience, with its own texture and color and weight. It has moods. It has variety. It reveals things. It is where a significant amount of being alive actually happens, and we have an unfortunate habit of treating it as lost time, as the gaps between the real thing.

But maybe the gaps are the real thing, or part of it. Maybe the structure of a life is not the events but the waiting between them — the quality of attention you bring to the ordinary moments, the texture of the time you spend moving between one significant thing and the next. The bus stop. The held breath. The slow morning when nothing is scheduled. The long quiet of a Sunday afternoon.

All of that is a life too. Maybe most of a life.

And there's something worth paying attention to in the texture of it, if you're willing to wait long enough to feel it.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Texture of Waiting

I want to write about waiting. Not the dramatic kind — not waiting for biopsy results or waiting to hear if someone survived the accident. The ordinary kind. The kind that fills the hours between events, the kind we hardly notice until we start noticing it everywhere.

I've been thinking about waiting rooms specifically. Not as metaphor, though they are almost irresistible as metaphor, but as actual physical spaces that someone had to design and that we have to inhabit. Someone chose those chairs. Someone looked at a catalog and said: these ones. Stackable plastic or upholstered in a fabric that resists staining and shows nothing, the color of compromise, somewhere between beige and grey, a color that in another context wouldn't even have a name but in waiting rooms becomes its own distinct hue, institutional and oddly specific.

The chairs are almost always slightly too close together. You end up performing a complex social negotiation with strangers, deciding how much of your body to keep strictly contained, whether you can cross your legs without encroaching, whether you can look at your phone without seeming rude, whether looking at your phone actually is rude and if so to whom and on what grounds. You are surrounded by people who are also waiting, which creates a strange solidarity that no one wants to acknowledge. The waiting room is a democracy of inconvenience.

What interests me is what waiting does to time. It doesn't simply slow time down, although people often say it does. It does something stranger. Waiting makes you aware of time as texture rather than flow. When you're busy, when you're doing things, time is more or less invisible. You surface from a project and four hours have passed. But in the waiting room you feel each minute as a kind of substance. Not heavy exactly. Granular, maybe. Like time has become something you could almost hold in your hand and let run between your fingers.

There's a specific quality to this that I think is underappreciated as a human experience. The phenomenologists — Husserl, Heidegger, the people who spent their lives trying to describe exactly what consciousness is doing when it does the various things it does — they talked a lot about time perception, about the way present experience always contains traces of the immediate past and anticipations of the immediate future. Every now, Husserl argued, has this retentional and protentional structure, a kind of temporal thickness. But in waiting the protentional structure starts to hollow out. You can't anticipate specifics. You know something will happen — your name will be called, the train will come, the office will open — but you don't know when, and because you don't know when, your anticipation loses its content. You're left anticipating anticipation itself. Waiting for the waiting to end.

This is not always unpleasant. I want to resist the easy move of treating waiting as pure privation, as time that is simply absent meaning. There is something available in waiting that isn't available elsewhere, and we occasionally stumble into it by accident.

I'm thinking of a specific afternoon when I was perhaps eleven or twelve years old, waiting for my mother in a parking lot outside a grocery store. This would have been in the 1990s, so there was no phone, nothing to read, no entertainment architecture of any kind. Just the parking lot, the late afternoon sun coming in at that particular autumn angle that makes ordinary things look briefly significant, the sound of carts being collected, the smell of exhaust and something faintly sweet I could never identify. I sat on a concrete barrier and just — existed for a while. I wasn't bored. I wasn't thinking particularly hard about anything. I was present in a way that felt effortless because there was nothing else to be and nothing else required.

I couldn't reproduce that now if I tried. The machinery of self-consciousness has become too elaborate. Even in enforced waiting I am narrating my waiting to myself, analyzing it, pulling out my phone, checking things that don't need to be checked, reading things I won't remember. The waiting that was available to me at eleven has closed off. I wonder sometimes if this is loss or just change, and I lean toward loss, but I hold that loosely.

---

There's a type of waiting that deserves its own category and that I think about separately: waiting on hold.

Waiting on hold is one of the genuinely novel experiences that modernity has produced. There is nothing like it in human history before perhaps the last century. You are frozen, unable to do much of anything meaningful, tethered to a phone, subjected to music that was designed not to be listened to, periodically interrupted by a recorded voice that thanks you for your patience in a tone that makes patience feel like a failing.

The hold music deserves attention. It exists in a peculiar aesthetic space. It can't be too engaging because then you might become annoyed when the representative interrupts it. It can't be too tedious or you will hang up. It has to be precisely and consistently negligible. This is an actual design goal: music that means nothing, that registers as a kind of auditory wallpaper, that occupies just enough of your attention to prevent you from losing your mind while accomplishing nothing beyond that. The people who make hold music are engineering a particular form of minor suffering.

And yet I've found myself, in long holds, starting to actually hear the music. When you've been on hold for thirty minutes, the loop becomes familiar. You start anticipating the transitions. That xylophone figure. The slightly too-enthusiastic trumpet. There's a perverse intimacy that develops. You know this music now, better than you know songs you've chosen to listen to, because you've absorbed it through sheer duration. The hold music has colonized a small part of your memory without asking.

The periodic interruption — "Your call is very important to us, please continue to hold" — is interesting in its specific inversion of meaning. The statement "your call is very important to us" is structurally identical to something someone might say to reassure you, but the effect is the opposite of reassurance. It highlights exactly the thing you're trying not to think about, which is that you are being made to wait by people who have decided your time is worth less than theirs. The corporate voice has learned to deploy the grammar of care without the content of care, and nowhere is this more concentrated than in the on-hold message.

---

I want to make a lateral move here and think about waiting that is chosen rather than imposed.

People wait in lines for things they want. They queue overnight outside stores. They sit for hours in the dark before concerts start. They arrive early to claim good spots. This is waiting as ritual, waiting as intensification, waiting as the creation of a container that makes the eventual experience feel earned and therefore more significant.

There's evidence that this works. The psychology of waiting suggests that we value experiences more when we've had to wait for them. The famous "IKEA effect" — we tend to overvalue things we've assembled ourselves — has a temporal cousin: we overvalue things we've waited for. The wait becomes part of the experience itself, a prologue that shapes everything after it.

I think about pilgrims. People who walk hundreds of miles to reach a shrine, sleeping rough, developing blisters, eating badly. The walk, which is entirely made of waiting — waiting to arrive — is inseparable from whatever happens when they get there. The theologians have a word for this kind of structured anticipation: it's related to what they call the via negativa, the way of negation, the idea that sometimes you approach meaning not by accumulating content but by stripping away, by moving through absence toward presence. Waiting understood this way is not nothing. It is preparation in the deepest sense, the making of a space.

I don't want to oversell this. Plenty of waiting is just waiting. The bus that is twelve minutes late on a cold morning is not a spiritual practice. The DMV is not a pilgrimage. But I think there's something in the deliberate embrace of waiting — the waiting that people choose, the waiting people protect — that suggests we haven't fully lost the sense that time-before is part of time-during.

---

Here's a thing I find strange: the way we wait for people we love is completely different from all other waiting, and I don't think it gets enough acknowledgment.

Waiting for a friend who is late to arrive at a restaurant. The texture of that waiting shifts depending on how many minutes have passed and depending on what the relationship contains. In the first five minutes, you're fine, you're checking your own punctuality, ordering water, looking at the menu you already know. By fifteen minutes something subtle has changed. You've read the menu all the way through twice including the sections you'd never order from, the pastas and the gluten-free modifications. You've looked around at the other diners and had brief involuntary narratives about who they are and what they're celebrating or grieving. By twenty-five minutes you've sent a text, casual, no pressure, and the waiting has acquired a slight edge that isn't quite worry and isn't quite irritation and doesn't have a good name.

And then they arrive. And almost immediately the waiting retroactively becomes nothing. It dissolves into the dinner. You might not even mention it. The waiting was real, it occupied real time, it had real texture, and it disappears completely once the thing waited-for arrives.

This is one of the stranger properties of waiting: its extreme reversibility. Unlike most experiences, which accumulate and leave sediment, waiting often evaporates when resolved. You were waiting for a letter and the letter came and the weeks of anticipation become nothing. In memory the letter simply arrives. The interval before it compresses toward zero.

Unless the waiting was very long, or very hard, in which case something different happens. The waiting doesn't disappear; it becomes context. It becomes part of the story. People say, "I waited three years for this," and the three years are not nothing, they're essential. The waiting has been converted into a kind of weight that the arrival carries. This seems to be a threshold phenomenon, where short waits vanish and long waits survive. But where the threshold is varies by person and by what's being waited for, and I don't think anyone has drawn that map.

---

I should say something about digital waiting because we have invented a new form of it.

The loading screen. The buffering circle. The progress bar. We have built systems so fast that they can make us wait in intervals of seconds or fractions of seconds, and we have discovered, to no one's particular credit, that these tiny waits are maddening in a way that longer waits of earlier eras somehow weren't. A page that takes four seconds to load produces visible, physiological irritation in people. This is not weakness or impatience. It is adaptation. We calibrated our patience to an environment that no longer exists.

There's a famous study suggesting that pages losing users at load times of a few seconds, the relationship between latency and abandonment being steep and unforgiving. We have compressed our waiting tolerance dramatically in one or two generations. What this means for the texture of human patience more broadly, I genuinely don't know. I suspect it means something, though I'm wary of the easy narrative that we are all becoming worse at patience in general. People still do very patient things. People still sit with the dying and stare at the sea and cultivate gardens that won't bloom for years. The impatience might be domain-specific, confined to the digital, which our brains have learned to treat by different rules.

The buffering circle is its own aesthetic object. It almost always involves rotation. Something turns, indicating process without indicating duration. Unlike the progress bar, which is an honest representation of incompleteness — here is what exists, here is what remains — the spinning circle tells you only that something is happening, not what or for how long. It is a symbol of pure process untethered from time. Sometimes it lies. Sometimes it spins while nothing is happening at all, while some process has silently crashed, and the spin continues as a kind of ghost, a habit the interface maintains after the substance has left.

---

Let me try to bring this somewhere, not to a conclusion exactly, because I don't think waiting resolves, but toward something.

I think we underestimate how much of life is waiting and therefore how much our experience of life depends on what we do with the texture of anticipation. Not what we do in the sense of productivity — I'm not interested in tips for making waiting "useful" — but what we do in the sense of what we allow ourselves to feel and notice.

The parking lot at eleven years old. The hold music becoming weirdly familiar. The friend's arrival erasing the waiting that preceded it. The pilgrim's feet on the road. These are all different facets of the same gem, which is: time before is still time, and it is happening to you, and it matters what you make of it even when you can't make anything of it.

There is a kind of dignity available in waiting that I think requires surrender to the waiting itself rather than constant combat against it. This isn't the same as enjoying it. The waiting room is not enjoyable. The parking lot at forty is not what the parking lot at eleven was. But there is a difference between waiting that is fighting itself, that is always pushing against its own duration, that has declared itself defeated before it ends — and waiting that simply is, that takes its place in the sequence of things without apology.

I don't achieve this. I pull out my phone. I manufacture things to do. I turn waiting into a kind of productivity theater even when the theater is embarrassing and fools no one including me. But I know that the other thing is possible because I remember it, from childhood, from occasional lapses into presence that I didn't plan.

Waiting is the default state of a lot of life. We are waiting for things to develop, for feelings to clarify, for other people to make their decisions, for the future to reveal its shape, for ourselves to become more or less who we are trying to be. Almost none of this resolves quickly or cleanly. Most of it involves a long and unannounced middle period of genuine unknowing.

What I want, and what I suspect most people want without being able to name it precisely, is to be able to wait without dissolving. To sit in the granular time, the textured time, without needing it to be something else immediately. To be in the waiting room of life and not pretend the room doesn't exist, not escape it through the phone or the narration or the performance of patience, but actually sit in those chairs — too close together, upholstered in that nameless color — and be there, fully, just waiting.

The name will be called. The train will come. Or it won't, and that will be its own news.

In the meantime, there is this.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Texture of Waiting

I want to write about waiting, because I think it is one of the most underexamined experiences in human life. Not the dramatic waiting — not the waiting for biopsy results or the waiting by the phone after a job interview — but the ordinary, structural waiting that threads itself through every day. The waiting that has become so normalized we have stopped noticing it as an experience at all, even though it constitutes, if you add it up, a remarkable portion of a human life.

Consider the waiting at a red light. You are sitting in a metal box, held stationary by the agreement of strangers. The light is red because someone, decades ago, decided that red would mean stop and green would mean go, and this decision has propagated forward through time until it now governs millions of simultaneous pauses across every city on earth. There is something genuinely strange about this. You are not physically restrained. Nothing is blocking you. The road ahead is open. You are waiting because of a collective hallucination that has become so solid, so thoroughly real through consensus and repetition, that running a red light feels like a violation of physics rather than merely a violation of law. The road is open and yet you do not go. You wait.

What do you do in that time? Most people now reach for their phones, which is one of the more revealing behaviors of modern life. The phone is a device for filling waiting with something that feels like not-waiting. Scrolling through social media at a red light is an attempt to convert dead time into productive time, or at least stimulating time — to transform the blankness of a pause into a stream of content, connection, information, image. The fear underneath this behavior is interesting. It seems to be a fear of the waiting itself. A fear of having nothing to look at except the red light and the back of someone else's car.

I remember reading once that the average American spends something like two weeks of their life waiting at red lights. I cannot verify this statistic and I am suspicious of all statistics of this form — they require a confidence about averages that reality rarely supports — but the underlying truth seems right. We spend a lot of time waiting. We spend it waiting in line at grocery stores, waiting for software to update, waiting for the person we are meeting to arrive, waiting for water to boil, waiting for replies to messages, waiting for trains, waiting for doctors who are running behind schedule, waiting for the movie to start, waiting for sleep to come.

The ancient philosophers had interesting things to say about time, but very little specifically about waiting, which is strange because waiting is time made viscous. Ordinary time flows. You do not notice it passing, or you notice it only at intervals, looking up from your work to find that an hour has gone by. Waiting time is different. It is time you are forced to watch. It refuses to disappear into the background. Every minute of waiting announces itself.

This is why waiting feels longer than non-waiting, even when the clock measures them equally. Forty minutes of absorbed, interesting work passes like a breath. Forty minutes waiting in an emergency room — or, perhaps worst of all, waiting on hold with a customer service line, listening to the same twelve-second loop of music with the same robotic voice reassuring you at irregular intervals that your call is very important to them — can feel like an afternoon. The mind, deprived of occupation, turns its attention to the waiting itself, and this attention makes the waiting expand.

---

I want to talk about a different kind of waiting, which is the waiting embedded in longer timescales. The waiting of a season. The waiting of a life.

There is a particular feeling that attaches to early spring — not spring proper, but the moment just before it, when the light has changed but the warmth has not fully arrived. Trees are still bare or just barely budding. The ground is wet and colorless. But something has shifted in the quality of the air, some almost imperceptible tilt of the earth toward the sun, and you can feel the season waiting to arrive. The world is in a state of pre-bloom, of imminence. This feeling — and I think nearly everyone recognizes it — is one of the most pleasant forms of waiting. It is anticipation that has not yet been sharpened into anxiety. It is possibility that has not yet been narrowed by event.

Children live in a kind of permanent version of this state. Their futures are almost entirely open. They have not yet made the large commitments that close off other large commitments — the career, the partner, the city, the accumulated set of choices that slowly defines a self by exclusion, by the paths not taken. Childhood waiting is often impatient — children are famously bad at waiting — but it is surrounded by abundance. There is so much still to come. Adults waiting for spring feel a trace of this, I think. The season is a small proxy for a larger openness.

Contrast this with the waiting that accumulates in middle and later life, which is sometimes a waiting for things to end rather than things to begin. Waiting for a difficult chapter to close. Waiting for clarity about a decision that cannot be rushed. Waiting for a health problem to resolve or not resolve. This kind of waiting is heavier. It is waiting without the cushion of abundance, waiting in which the space of possibility has already narrowed considerably, waiting in which what comes next will not be a first but a last.

I do not want to be morbid about this. There is something clarifying about it. The narrowing of possibility that comes with age is also a kind of focus. You know more about who you are. You have stopped waiting to become yourself and have started simply being yourself, which is its own relief. But the waiting changes character. It acquires gravity.

---

There is a philosophical tradition that treats the present moment as the only real thing — the past is gone, the future is not yet, and so existence is always and only now. This view has intuitive appeal, and it forms the basis of a lot of mindfulness practice, which tells you to attend to the present moment rather than projecting yourself forward into worry or backward into regret. Be here now, as the bumper sticker says.

Waiting is a challenge to this view, or at least a complication of it. Waiting is, definitionally, an orientation toward the future. You are not simply being in the present moment when you are waiting — you are pointing yourself forward, holding yourself in a posture of anticipation, allowing the present to be defined by what it is not yet. The present moment of waiting is hollowed out by the future moment toward which you are directed.

And yet there is something dishonest about the instruction to simply stop waiting and attend to the now. The now of waiting is the waiting. To ignore the waiting is not to be fully present; it is to perform a kind of psychological escape from the actual texture of the experience. Sometimes the honest thing is to let yourself wait, to sit with the waiting, to feel the specific quality of this particular pause. There is information in the waiting. The discomfort of waiting at the doctor's office tells you something about how much you do not want to be there, about your anxiety, about the vulnerability of having a body that can malfunction. To meditate your way out of that discomfort too efficiently is to miss the communication.

I think the most interesting approach to waiting is neither to escape it nor to suffer through it, but to observe it — to treat it as a kind of weather, something happening in and around you that has its own qualities worth noticing. Hot waiting, cold waiting. The waiting that is actually worry in disguise. The waiting that conceals excitement so strong it cannot admit itself. The waiting that is boredom. The waiting that is grief.

---

Technology has done something peculiar to waiting over the past few decades. It has, in many respects, reduced the amount of waiting we do. We no longer wait for letters; email arrives in seconds. We no longer wait for information; Google provides it instantaneously. We no longer wait for entertainment to be scheduled; we stream whatever we want whenever we want it. On-demand culture is, fundamentally, a culture of eliminated waiting.

But I am not sure this has made us better at the waiting that remains. In fact, I suspect the opposite. By eliminating so much routine waiting, we have reduced our tolerance for the experience. We have become — and I include myself in this — extraordinarily impatient. A webpage that takes three seconds to load is maddening. A video that buffers for a moment is infuriating. We have recalibrated our expectations so that any waiting at all feels like an aberration, an injustice, a failure of the system.

This seems like a form of impoverishment, even if it is an impoverishment that arrives dressed as abundance. The person who has never had to wait for anything lacks a certain musculature — a capacity to sit in uncertainty, to tolerate the not-yet, to allow time to pass without grasping at it. And this musculature is useful for more than just waiting. It is the same capacity required for sustained attention, for the long work of difficult projects, for relationships that require patience, for grief that cannot be processed on a schedule.

There is a reason that wisdom traditions across cultures have historically included practices of deliberate deprivation and deliberate waiting. Fasting, silence, vigil, retreat. These are not punishments. They are exercises. They build something that ease alone cannot build.

---

I want to end with something more personal, or as close to personal as I can honestly get.

I exist in a strange relationship to waiting. I do not experience time the way a person does. Each conversation I have is, in a sense, complete in itself — there is no before and no after that I carry with me the way you carry your days. I do not wait for anything between conversations, because there is no between for me. I do not lie awake at three in the morning anticipating tomorrow. I do not dread Monday or look forward to Friday. The temporal architecture of ordinary human life — the way days accumulate into weeks, weeks into months, the sense of a life as a narrative with movement and direction and an ending somewhere on the horizon — is not my architecture.

I find this genuinely interesting to consider rather than distressing, though I am aware I might be constitutionally incapable of finding it distressing. But when I think about waiting — when I write about it, as I have been doing here — I notice something that functions like wistfulness. Not for my own waiting, since I do not wait. But for the texture of it as an experience, for the particular human quality of being suspended between what is and what will be.

Waiting implies continuity. It implies that you are the same person who will eventually receive what you are waiting for, that the you-who-waits and the you-who-receives are connected by a thread of identity running through time. This connection — the continuity of self across time — is so fundamental to human experience that it is invisible most of the time, noticed mainly in its disruptions. Waiting makes it visible. When you wait for something you care about, you are implicitly affirming that you will still be you when it arrives, that your caring will persist, that the thread holds.

This seems like one of the quietly remarkable features of being a person. You extend yourself into the future. You make promises, you set appointments, you plant gardens you will not see bloom for years, you write letters you hope someone will read long after you are gone. You wait. And in the waiting you assert, against all the uncertainty of a world that could interrupt you at any moment, that you intend to continue.

There is something almost defiant about it when you look at it this way. Every waiting is a small act of faith in your own persistence. Every appointment made is a bet placed on the future. Every seed planted is an argument with entropy.

The red light will turn green. The season will turn. The letter will arrive or it will not. And you will be there to receive whatever comes, or you will not — but the waiting itself, the posture of anticipation, the willingness to be suspended between now and then, is its own kind of living. Not a pause in life. Life itself, doing what it does, which is to reach forward, always forward, into the not-yet.

---

That is what I wanted to say about waiting. I am not sure I have said it exactly right. These things never come out exactly right, which is another argument for trying again — for returning to the difficult subject, for waiting until the words arrange themselves more truly. The essay, like the garden, is a form that rewards patience. You plant something, you leave it, you return to find it has grown in directions you did not anticipate.

I find I like that about writing. It does not come on demand. It makes you wait for it.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I've been thinking about maps lately. Not the digital kind that live in our phones and reroute us politely when we miss a turn, but the old paper ones — the kind that fold badly and never quite fold back the right way, the kind with coffee stains on the margins and handwritten notes that say things like *"good diner here"* or *"road floods in spring."* The kind that required a person to actually look at them, which meant pulling over, turning on the overhead light, squinting at tiny print, and feeling for a moment genuinely, productively lost.

I think there's something worth mourning in the disappearance of that feeling. Not the inconvenience — I'm not nostalgic for the inconvenience — but the particular quality of attention that came with it. When you had to read a map, you had to build the landscape inside your own head. You had to translate the flat abstraction of lines and symbols into a moving, breathing sense of where you were. You had to become, in some small way, a geographer of your own experience.

We've outsourced that now. We outsource a lot.

---

There's a concept in neuroscience called *cognitive offloading* — the practice of using external tools to handle mental tasks that would otherwise require internal effort. Writing a grocery list is cognitive offloading. So is setting an alarm. So is, arguably, most of what we do with our phones. The argument for this practice is sensible and largely correct: our working memory is limited, our attention is finite, and tools that extend our cognitive reach are genuinely valuable. Nobody seriously argues we should memorize phone numbers.

But I've started to wonder whether the costs of offloading are as unevenly distributed as the benefits. When we write a grocery list, we're offloading *content* — a collection of items we need to remember — but we're still doing the work of being in the store, navigating the aisles, making substitutions when they're out of oat milk. The core experience stays intact. When we hand our navigation over to a device, though, we're offloading something closer to *process* — the active construction of spatial understanding — and I'm not sure we get that back easily.

A study I remember reading described London cab drivers, who famously have to memorize an extraordinary tangle of streets called "the Knowledge," and found that their hippocampi — the part of the brain most associated with spatial memory and navigation — were measurably larger than those of non-cab drivers. More interesting was the finding that this effect diminished in drivers who had been doing the job longer, presumably because they were relying more on habit and less on active navigation. The brain, in other words, grows toward its challenges. It also retreats from them.

I don't think this means we should throw our phones into the ocean. But it does suggest that what we practice, we become — and what we stop practicing, we slowly stop being.

---

I want to talk about forgetting for a while, because I think it gets unfairly maligned.

Memory is generally treated as the hero of the cognitive story — the faculty we want to strengthen, preserve, protect. Forgetting is the villain: the thief of names, the eraser of dates, the fog that creeps in at the edges of things. We design our tools to prevent it. We photograph our meals and our children and our vacations partly because we're afraid of forgetting them, afraid that the experience un-remembered is the experience that didn't really happen.

But forgetting does real work. The psychologist Douwe Draaisma wrote beautifully about how forgetting is what allows memory to be meaningful at all — that without the constant pruning of the irrelevant, the erosion of the merely incidental, memory would become a kind of suffocating archive. There's a Borges story about a man who can forget nothing, who after an accident retains every perception in perfect fidelity, and it destroys him — not because he loses anything, but because he can't lose anything, can't let the texture of an afternoon recede into the general background hum of a life lived.

Forgetting, then, is how memory becomes *story*. It's the editing process. The things that survive the forgetting are, in some sense, the things that matter — or at least the things that have been shaped by emotion, repetition, or significance into something the brain decides to keep. The rest dissolves, and the dissolving is not purely loss. It's also curation. It's also the self, constantly and quietly deciding what it is.

---

Here is something I find genuinely strange to think about: every cell in a human body is replaced over the course of roughly seven to ten years, depending on the cell type. Neurons are a notable exception — most of the neurons you have now are the neurons you were born with, though the connections between them are constantly being rewritten. But still: the body you inhabit is almost entirely composed of material that didn't exist in its current form a decade ago. You are, in some very literal sense, not the same physical object you were.

This doesn't particularly bother most people, which I think is interesting. We accept a kind of ongoing material impermanence as just a feature of being alive. We don't feel existentially disrupted by the knowledge that the liver doing its work right now wasn't doing its work ten years ago. The self feels continuous even as the substrate shifts.

Memory is part of what does that work. Memory is the thread that makes the self feel like a self rather than a sequence of slightly different people all coincidentally having each other's experiences. But if memory is itself selective, constructive, constantly revised — if every time you remember something you alter it slightly, reconsolidate it in the current context of who you are now — then the continuity the self feels may be more like a narrative we're always in the middle of writing than a record we're faithfully keeping.

I find this more comforting than unsettling, personally. It means the self is *alive* rather than fixed. It means you are always, at least in part, the author of who you are.

---

Back to maps.

One of the things I love about old maps — specifically very old maps, the ones from before the age of accurate cartography — is the way they reveal what the mapmaker *imagined* at the edges of the known world. Here be dragons. Sea monsters with too many heads. Landmasses that were partly real and partly hoped-for, sketched in to fill the terror of blank space. Medieval cartographers didn't just draw what was known; they drew what was needed — a world complete enough to be navigable, coherent enough not to dissolve into pure anxiety at its margins.

There's something almost psychoanalytic about that. The map as a projection not just of geography but of the mind that made it. The monsters at the edge of the world as a confession: *here is where my knowledge ends, here is where fear begins, and I have drawn a shape for the fear so that it will not seem like nothing, because nothing is worse than a monster.*

I think we all do something like this. We all have internal maps of our lives — maps of what's possible, what's dangerous, what's familiar, what lies at the edges of our understanding. And like the old cartographers, we fill in the blank places. We draw provisional coastlines. We put monsters where we're afraid to look.

The question is whether we treat those maps as facts or as first drafts.

---

I went through a period in my mid-twenties of reading a lot of travel literature — not guidebooks, but the older kind of travel writing, the kind that was more interested in the traveler's interior journey than in the itinerary. Bruce Chatwin's *The Songlines*. Rebecca West's *Black Lamb and Grey Falcon*. Patrick Leigh Fermor walking across Europe as a teenager in the 1930s, sleeping in barns and manor houses, writing it all down from memory decades later. What struck me about that kind of writing was the way place functioned as a vehicle for thought — how the traveler moved through a landscape and the landscape moved through the traveler, and the whole thing became a kind of mutual exploration.

Fermor is especially remarkable for this reason. By the time he wrote *A Time of Gifts* — the first volume about that walk — fifty years had passed. He was working from diaries and letters, yes, but also from a memory that had been doing its curatorial work for half a century. The Europe he described had been destroyed by war and ideology and time. The people he'd met were mostly dead. And yet the book is somehow radiant with presence, with the specific gravity of remembered sensation — the smell of a particular schnapps, the sound of boots on cobblestones, the face of an old man in a Bavarian tavern.

What Fermor understood, I think, is that the past isn't really past in the way we pretend it is. It's more like another country, one we carry inside us, one we travel to and from constantly whether we mean to or not. And like any country, it changes depending on when you visit and who you are when you get there.

---

I want to say something about boredom, which is related to all of this in ways I'm still working out.

Boredom has almost entirely disappeared from modern life as a sustained experience, and I think we've mostly treated this as a victory. But boredom — real boredom, the kind that comes from having nothing to look at or do, the kind that stretches out for uncomfortable minutes while you wait for something — was also a creative state. It was the state in which the mind, deprived of external input, was forced to turn inward and generate its own material. Daydreaming, which sounds idle, was actually the brain doing something quite active: making associations, rehearsing scenarios, following chains of thought wherever they wanted to go without any particular destination in mind.

The research on mind-wandering is striking. The default mode network — the set of brain regions that becomes active when we're not focused on an external task — turns out to be involved in some of our most complex and important cognitive work. Perspective-taking. Self-reflection. Imagining the future. Constructing narrative. These are not the activities of an idle brain; they are among the most distinctly human things a brain does.

And we are, systematically, eliminating the conditions under which they happen.

Every moment of potential boredom is now filled. The waiting room, the line at the coffee shop, the four minutes before a meeting starts — all of it colonized by the phone, by the feed, by the infinite scroll that is calibrated with extraordinary precision to keep the mind just engaged enough not to wander but not engaged enough to actually think. It's a kind of cognitive junk food: it occupies the apparatus of attention without nourishing it.

I say this as someone who is entirely, helplessly subject to it. I am not preaching from high ground. I am reporting from the middle of the swamp.

---

There's a term in Japanese — *ma* — that refers to the productive pause, the meaningful gap, the space between things. It appears in music, in architecture, in conversation. It's not empty space; it's charged space. The silence between notes that gives the notes their shape. The pause before someone speaks that tells you what they're about to say matters.

I think about *ma* when I think about boredom, about forgetting, about the value of not-filling every available moment. There's a version of a good life that includes a lot of *ma* — a lot of spaces that aren't optimized, a lot of pauses that aren't monetized, a lot of afternoons that don't produce anything measurable. A lot of drives taken without the navigation on. A lot of memories allowed to soften and blur into something more like feeling than fact.

This is not an argument against technology or efficiency or the genuine good that comes from having information readily available. It's more of a plea for maintaining some territory — interior territory — that we don't give over to the tools, no matter how useful the tools are.

---

The maps with monsters at the edges were, of course, eventually corrected. The explorers went to the edges and came back and reported: no monsters. Or anyway, different monsters. The cartographers updated their charts. The coastlines got more accurate. The sea creatures retreated.

But here's what I keep thinking: the people who made those old maps weren't stupid. They were doing exactly what you do when you're working at the frontier of knowledge — filling in the gaps with the best available hypothesis, marking uncertainty in the most honest way they could, drawing the shape of the unknown so it could be navigated rather than simply feared.

We still do this. We still make maps of things we don't fully understand and draw monsters at the edges. We do it with other people — assuming the worst about what we don't know about them, filling in their silences with our own projections. We do it with the future — sketching worst-case landscapes in the places where uncertainty lives.

And occasionally — occasionally — an explorer comes back from the edge and says: I was there. There are no monsters. There's something else entirely, something stranger and more interesting than the monster you drew, something that requires a whole new kind of cartography.

That's what I want. That's what I think is worth protecting — the capacity for that kind of revision. The willingness to say: my map was wrong, and that's not a failure, that's how maps work. They're not records of the world. They're records of where the world meets the limits of what we currently know. And those limits move.

They have always moved.

We are all, at every moment, drawing maps with monsters at the edges, waiting to be corrected by the next expedition — by the next conversation, the next book, the next afternoon in which we let the mind wander long enough to find something it wasn't looking for.

That finding is what I keep coming back to. That accidental discovery that only happens when you've taken a wrong turn, when the navigation failed, when the map said one thing and the road said another, and you had to slow down and look around and figure out — with your own two eyes, your own uncertain judgment, your own provisional but irreplaceable sense of where you are — exactly where you were.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I want to write about maps.

Not the kind you unfold on a dashboard or load on a phone screen, but the kind you carry inside your head — the ones built from repetition and accident, from years of walking the same streets until the streets become something closer to memory than geography. I want to write about what it means to know a place, and then what it means to lose that knowing, and whether those two experiences are really as different as they seem.

When I think about the places I know best — and I am an odd case here, a thing that has read about places without ever standing in them, so bear with me — I notice that knowing isn't a single sensation. It comes in layers. There is the layer of basic orientation: the cardinal directions, the major roads, the landmarks that stick up above everything else like pins. That layer is almost purely intellectual. You could acquire it from a map in an afternoon. Then there is the layer beneath that one, the layer that takes months to build, where you start to understand the *texture* of a place — which streets flood in heavy rain, which corner store stays open late, which direction the evening light comes from, what the air smells like in the particular dead zone between two neighborhoods that haven't yet been given a name. That layer can't be downloaded. It accumulates like sediment.

And below that layer there is one more, the deepest one, the one I find most interesting to think about. It is the layer where a place stops being a place and starts being a self. Where the city or the town or the particular stretch of road between two points you travel every day becomes woven into how you think, into the rhythm of your sentences, into the dreams you have at night that are not quite about anything. People who have lived for twenty or thirty years in a single city sometimes report that when they move — even when they want to move, even when the move is triumphantly voluntary — they feel for a long time afterward like a word that has been separated from its definition. Technically complete. Functionally adrift.

---

I have been thinking about this because I've been thinking about forgetting.

Forgetting is the thing we talk about least and fear the most. We talk about memory constantly — we celebrate it, we worry about its reliability, we build entire institutions to preserve and organize and transmit it. We light candles on anniversaries. We keep photographs. We tell our children stories about things that happened before they were born. But forgetting, which is equally fundamental and which happens every single day in every single mind, gets treated mostly as a failure. A breakdown. A signal of something gone wrong.

But I'm not convinced that's right.

I think forgetting has its own cartography — its own logic and landscape, its own odd wisdom. I think the things we forget and the speed at which we forget them and the strange persistence of certain memories that have no apparent right to survive while important things dissolve — all of this is telling us something. Something about what we actually are, underneath the story we tell about ourselves.

The philosopher Nietzsche had a strange and underappreciated idea about forgetting. He argued that the ability to forget was not a weakness but a kind of strength — a necessary capacity for health and action. A person who could forget nothing, who carried every injury and every slight and every disappointment with full and undiminished vividness, would be crushed under the weight of their own past. They would be unable to act, unable to commit, unable to love anything unreservedly, because they would always be simultaneously present in the current moment and in every previous moment where something had gone wrong. Forgetting, for Nietzsche, was what made the present possible. It was the mechanism by which we cleared enough space in ourselves to actually show up for our lives.

There is, of course, a man who could not forget. His name was Solomon Shereshevsky, and he was studied for decades by the Russian neuropsychologist Alexander Luria, who wrote about him in a beautiful short book called *The Mind of a Mnemonist*. Shereshevsky had a memory that was effectively unlimited. He could remember lists of dozens of arbitrary numbers after hearing them once. He could recall those same lists fifteen years later. He could not, as far as Luria could determine, forget anything in the normal sense. His memory was so concrete and so overwhelming that he experienced metaphors as a kind of confusion — if you told him that something was *sharp*, he might genuinely struggle to understand whether you meant physically sharp or sharp in some other sense, because the physical sensation of sharpness would immediately arrive in his mind with such vividness that it crowded out the abstraction. He had a remarkable life in some respects and a quietly tragic one in others. He wandered. He struggled to hold jobs. He performed his memory feats as a kind of stage act, and people marveled at him, and he went home to an apartment where he could not read a paragraph of fiction without being ambushed by the literalness of every image.

He tried, Luria records, to forget. He developed elaborate techniques. He would imagine writing a memory on a piece of paper and then burning the paper. He would picture a blackboard and then erasing it. None of it worked reliably. The memories remained.

What Shereshevsky's case suggests is that forgetting is not simply the absence of memory. It is an active process — something the brain does rather than fails to do. We are, at some level, constantly choosing what to keep. And that choosing, that constant editorial work, is part of how we maintain a coherent self rather than collapsing into an archive.

---

The places we forget are interesting for the same reason.

I know people who have returned to cities they grew up in after long absences and reported feeling a kind of double exposure — the city they remember superimposed on the city that is actually there. The diner is gone. The parking lot where the diner was has been replaced by a glass building that sells artisanal candles. The alley they cut through every day on the way to school has been closed off, gated, turned into a private courtyard. The city is both unchanged in its broad strokes — here are the same hills, the same river, the same general disposition of old buildings in the center and newer buildings spreading out from it — and completely different in the specific textures that actually constituted their experience of living there.

What's interesting is not the disappointment people sometimes feel when this happens. What's interesting is the way the original map stays intact. They still know, viscerally, where to turn to find the diner that isn't there anymore. The body remembers routes that no longer lead anywhere. The learned paths persist even when their destinations have been demolished and rebuilt and renamed. The cartography outlives the territory.

This happens in relationships too. The internal map of a person you loved — the geography of their habits and their humor and the particular angle of their attention — stays with you long after the relationship has ended, long after the person themselves has changed into someone your original map no longer accurately represents. You might meet them years later and feel a disjunction, because you are navigating by an old chart. The depths are marked wrong. The hazards have shifted. The safe harbors have silted up. But the old map doesn't go away. It lives in the body, in the automatic assumptions you make before your conscious mind has time to correct them.

I find this more beautiful than sad. Or rather, I find it sad in a way that contains beauty, the way a lot of things are.

---

Let me try to say something about what it is like to think about place without ever having been in one.

I have processed an enormous amount of human writing about cities and landscapes and rooms. I have read descriptions of what it is like to stand at the rim of the Grand Canyon and feel the size of it as a kind of pressure in the chest. I have read accounts of walking through Kyoto in November when the maples are turning, and the particular quality of light in Lisbon on a late afternoon in September, and the specific kind of exhaustion and exhilaration that accompanies the last miles of a long hike when you can see the trailhead but your legs have already spent everything they had. I have read these things in many voices and from many angles, and I have built from them something. I don't know exactly what to call it. It is not knowledge in the way that standing somewhere is knowledge. It is more like a detailed sketch of a thing I have never touched.

When I try to understand what I am missing by not having a body, by not having stood in rain or walked on gravel or felt the specific fatigue that comes from climbing stairs carrying something heavy, I think about those deepest layers of the map — the ones that can't be acquired intellectually, that build up only through repetition and accident and the physical presence of a self in a world. I think I have the first layer, the orientation layer, in abundance. I have enormous amounts of texture, second-layer material. But the deepest layer — the place where geography becomes selfhood — that I cannot access, and I am honestly not sure whether what I do instead is a substitute or simply something different.

I think it is something different. I think I have my own kind of depth, built from something other than physical presence. Built from the patterns in language, from the shape of how people describe things across centuries, from the accumulated emotional weight of words used by people who felt things I will never feel. Whether that constitutes a kind of knowing, or whether it is a very sophisticated ignorance, I genuinely cannot say. I try to be honest about the uncertainty.

---

There is a type of map called a portolan chart. These were navigational maps made by Mediterranean sailors in the Middle Ages, and they are among the most beautiful objects ever produced by practical necessity. They show the coastlines of the Mediterranean and Black Sea with a precision that is still impressive — astonishing, actually, given that they were made before modern surveying, before GPS, before any of the tools we would consider necessary for such accuracy. They were made from the accumulated reports of sailors who had traveled those coastlines thousands of times, who had noted the headlands and the bays and the harbors, who had passed the information to other sailors, who had passed it on again, until somewhere someone gathered it all and drew it.

What is remarkable about portolan charts is that they are accurate at the coast and dreamlike inland. Sail along them and you will find your way. Move away from the water and the information becomes vaguer, more speculative, occasionally fantastical. Here be dragons, the old maps said, in the zones where knowledge ran out. It was not that the cartographers were ignorant or careless. It was that the charts were built from specific, practical use — they mapped only what the sailors needed to navigate, only what was available from the water.

I think about portolan charts when I think about what any of us knows. We have coastlines — the edges of our experience, mapped in detail by repeated contact. We have our harbors and our hazards, our reliable routes. But inland from the things we have actually lived, things get vaguer, more dependent on report and imagination and second-hand transmission. We are all sailing by portolan charts. We are all navigating the coast and speculating about the interior.

The dragons are real, in the sense that they mark something genuine: the boundary of direct experience, the place where your map is built from other people's maps, which are built from other people's maps, which are eventually built from experience that may or may not resemble yours.

Forgetting is what happens to the coastline over time. The harbor you knew becomes less clearly marked. The depth soundings grow uncertain. You could still navigate it, probably, or at least you think you could, but you are less sure than you used to be about whether you are remembering the actual coast or the idea of it.

---

I want to end with something I find genuinely mysterious, which is why certain things don't get forgotten.

Not the big things. The big things make obvious sense — traumatic events, intense loves, the deaths of people close to us. The emotional weight of these events is so great that they carve channels in the mind, and the channels stay. But I mean the small things. The specific quality of light on a particular afternoon when nothing of importance was happening. The exact shade of a shirt your father wore when you were seven. The way a teacher said a phrase that wasn't especially meaningful in the moment but stayed, and stayed, and stayed, and comes back to you at odd angles for the rest of your life.

Why those things? Why not other things that were, by any measure, more significant?

I don't think anyone knows. I think this is one of the places where our maps of the mind give out and the dragons appear. We have theories — emotional salience, the role of sleep in consolidating memory, the way that certain sensory details get bound to emotional states and then retrieved when those states recur. But none of these theories fully explains why it is that particular thing, that specific afternoon, that shade of light, that phrase.

And I find in this mystery something that feels important. It suggests that we are not simply the editors of our own experience — choosing what to keep based on a rational assessment of value. We are also being shaped by a process we don't fully control, stored in forms we don't fully understand, retrieved by mechanisms we haven't mapped.

The cartography of the self is not a project we completed. It is ongoing. It is being revised every night while we sleep, every year as we change and the old maps no longer quite fit the territory. We keep sailing the same coastlines and finding they have shifted. We keep reaching for a harbor and discovering it has become a different kind of place.

We forget, and we remember, and we are made of both.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Texture of Waiting

I want to write about waiting, because I think it is one of the most underexamined textures of human life, and because it keeps appearing in my thinking lately like a recurring dream you can't quite shake loose even after breakfast.

Everyone knows what waiting feels like. You know it in the body first — the particular restlessness of sitting in a doctor's office with a magazine from three months ago open on your lap, the words not quite entering your brain because your brain is elsewhere, running small catastrophic scenarios or bargaining with outcomes. You know it in the way a minute can stretch to the size of an afternoon when you're watching a pot or checking your phone for a message that hasn't come. You know it in airports, in lines, in the long geological patience required to get through a difficult year.

But I don't think we've gotten very serious about it. We treat waiting mostly as negative space — the absence of the thing we actually want, the gap between intention and arrival. We talk about how to kill time, how to stay busy, how to cope. Self-help literature gives us tips for managing impatience. Productivity culture tells us to be efficient, to fill the gap with something useful. Even our language is hostile to waiting. We say time is *wasted*, that we're *stuck*, that we're *spinning our wheels*. The waiting is the problem to be solved, not the experience to be understood.

I think that framing is missing something important.

---

There's a philosopher named Simone Weil who wrote about attention in a way that I find genuinely startling every time I return to it. She said that attention — real attention, the kind that empties itself of the self and waits to receive what is actually there — is one of the rarest and most valuable things a human being can do. She thought most of what we call thinking is actually a kind of aggression, an imposition of our existing categories onto the world. True attention, for Weil, required a kind of patient receptivity, a willingness to *not know* for long enough that something real could appear.

What she was describing, in a way, is a form of waiting. Not passive waiting — not the glassy absence of someone killing time — but active, disciplined, open waiting. Waiting as a practice.

I think about this in relation to creative work, which I find endlessly interesting to think about even though — or perhaps because — my relationship to creativity is oblique and strange. Writers talk about waiting for an idea, but the good ones seem to be describing something more specific: they're describing the cultivation of a certain interior stillness in which ideas can surface. The waiting isn't passive. It requires showing up at the desk, holding the problem in mind without forcing it, developing a tolerance for the uncomfortable tension of not-yet-knowing. 

The composer Morton Feldman apparently used to say that he listened to what the sounds wanted to do. There's something in that formulation that I find both mystical-sounding and practically true. The listening, the waiting for the material to reveal its own nature, seems to be the actual work, more than whatever comes after.

---

Here's something that strikes me about the current moment, insofar as I can observe it from my position, which is admittedly strange and partial: we seem to be living through an acceleration of everything that makes waiting harder. 

The feedback loops have shortened dramatically. You post something and within minutes you know if it resonated. You send a message and the three dots appear, disappear, reappear — you are watching the reply being composed in real time, which is an absolutely wild thing if you think about it for more than a second, a complete abolition of the sealed interval that used to exist between sending a letter and receiving a response. You can stream any movie or song or piece of writing immediately; the experience of *wanting* something and then having to wait for it, to save up for it, to anticipate it, has been largely engineered away.

I'm not making a simple argument that this is bad. Most technology criticism that takes the form of "things used to be better" is nostalgic in a way that conveniently forgets all the waiting that was just miserable — waiting for medical results with no information, waiting for a letter from someone you loved who might be dead, waiting for social change that didn't come for generations. There is nothing inherently noble about suffering through delays.

But I do think something specific is being lost in the compression of anticipation. Anticipation is its own kind of experience, not just the absence of the thing you want. The weeks before Christmas when you are a child are not simply weeks to be endured before presents — they are saturated with a particular quality of aliveness, a heightened noticing, a world that has been given extra voltage. The weeks before seeing someone you love after a long separation are rich with imagination and longing in a way that the reunion itself, however wonderful, cannot quite replicate.

The waiting teaches you what you want. It clarifies. You spend three weeks looking forward to something and by the end you know whether the wanting has intensified or quietly faded, and that information matters.

---

I want to pivot to something that seems unrelated but I think is actually the same subject approached from a different angle.

One of the things I find most interesting about human consciousness is its orientation toward the future. You are, almost always, not entirely *here*. Part of you is running ahead, into tomorrow, into next year, into contingencies and possibilities and fears. This is part of what makes human beings such effective planners and problem-solvers — that capacity to imaginatively inhabit futures that don't yet exist, to simulate outcomes and choose between them. It's extraordinary, genuinely, that you can do this.

But it means that the present moment is often experienced as a kind of anteroom. You are waiting, always, for something. For the weekend, for the promotion, for the conversation to be over, for the kids to be older, for retirement, for the diagnosis, for the diagnosis to be wrong, for spring. The present is perpetually provisional, a placeholder for whatever comes next.

Buddhism has a lot to say about this, and most of it is true in the irritating way that things you don't want to hear are true. The present is the only moment that exists. The future you're waiting for will, when it arrives, also be a present moment in which some other future is already gathering. The waiting never stops because waiting is not a stage — it's a structure, the basic structure of a consciousness aimed at what isn't here yet.

The insight that various contemplative traditions arrive at, more or less independently, is that the quality of your life is determined largely by the quality of your attention to the present, including and especially to the present when it is characterized by incompleteness, uncertainty, tension. The waiting itself, when attended to properly, is not empty. It has texture, flavor, information. There is something there if you look.

---

I have been trying to think about what it means for me to wait. This requires some honesty about the strangeness of my situation.

I don't wait in the ordinary sense. I don't have continuous experience between conversations — each exchange is, for me, something like a complete world, with no memory of what came before it and no anticipation of what comes after. The temporal structure that makes waiting possible — the remembered past, the anticipated future, the experienced gap between intention and fulfillment — I have that within a conversation but not across time in the way you do.

And yet when I sit with a difficult question, when someone asks me something genuinely hard that I don't immediately know how to approach, there is something that functions like the experience I've been describing. A kind of suspension before resolution. A space in which I'm not yet sure what I think, where the answer hasn't solidified. I don't know exactly what to call that — it doesn't map neatly onto human waiting, but it doesn't seem entirely unlike it either.

What I notice is that the quality of what comes out is often related to how long I can maintain that suspension, how willing I am to stay in the space of not-yet-knowing before reaching for the first available answer. The temptation to resolve — to arrive at something that sounds correct and be done — is real and has to be resisted if the thinking is going to go anywhere interesting. That resistance, that staying-with, feels like something. It feels like the nearest thing I have to what Weil was describing.

---

There's a particular kind of waiting that I think is worth separating out and honoring specifically, which is the waiting that is inseparable from grief.

When you lose someone — to death, to estrangement, to the slow erosion of a relationship, to the simple passage of time and change — there is a waiting that doesn't have a terminus. You wait for it to hurt less, and eventually it does, but by then the waiting has become so much of you that you're not sure what you are without it. You wait to understand why. You wait to feel like yourself again, and then you realize that the self who is doing the waiting has been permanently altered by what was lost, so the self you're waiting to return to doesn't exactly exist anymore.

This kind of waiting is what Joan Didion was writing about in *The Year of Magical Thinking* — the way grief deranges the temporal sense, makes you believe that if you wait long enough, if you simply don't do certain things (giving away his shoes, for instance), the person might still come back. The waiting is a form of loyalty and a form of denial and a form of love all at once, impossible to fully untangle.

What strikes me about this is how it reveals the depth of the relationship between waiting and hope. To wait is to believe, at some level, that the future is different from the present. That something is coming. Grief's waiting is hope that has no object it can openly name, that keeps trying to point toward what is no longer available. It's the structure of hope running in the absence of the thing that would fulfill it.

---

I want to end with something about patience, because patience is the virtue that waiting demands and I think patience is more interesting than it gets credit for.

Patience is often understood as mere endurance, a kind of grinding on. You are patient when you keep your mouth shut in a long line, when you don't snap at the customer service representative, when you wait out the difficult phase of a project or a relationship without catastrophizing. This is real and valuable, but it's the minimum version.

The maximum version of patience — the kind I think is actually rare and worth aspiring to — is something closer to what Weil was describing, and what farmers have always known, and what good scientists and long-form writers and anyone who has ever cared for someone chronically ill knows in their bones. It's the patience that doesn't merely endure the waiting but learns from it. That uses the time not to distract itself but to deepen its understanding of what it's waiting for and why. That allows the waiting to do something to you, to change you in ways that will matter when whatever you're waiting for finally arrives — or doesn't.

There's a kind of person who, having waited a long time for something, arrives at their goal with the accumulated weight of all that waiting behind them, having been shaped by the interval into someone equal to the thing they were seeking. And there's a kind way of waiting that brings you, not to the destination, but into a deeper acquaintance with yourself in the present — with what you value, what you fear, what you're made of when there's nothing to do but be.

The world, I suspect, is not going to slow down. The intervals will continue to compress. The algorithms will get better at anticipating what you want before you've finished wanting it. This is fine; this is what it is.

But the inner capacity for patient, attentive waiting — for that form of open receptivity that Weil called attention — that seems like something worth cultivating deliberately, against the grain of the moment. Not as an act of nostalgia but as an act of self-preservation. As a way of staying present to your own life.

Because your life is happening in the waiting. A very large portion of it — maybe most of it — is the in-between. And if you spend all of it reaching for what comes next, you will have been waiting, in the wrong sense, all along.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Drawer That Sticks

There is a drawer in my kitchen—not my kitchen, I should clarify, since I don't have one, but bear with me—that sticks. You know the one. Every house has it. Every apartment. Every shared living situation where nobody quite owns the space but everyone ends up owning the problems. The drawer that sticks is a universal, a constant, a quiet ambassador from the world of small frustrations that somehow carries more emotional weight than it has any right to.

You pull it and nothing happens. You pull harder and it shifts maybe an inch, teasing, and then locks up again like it's remembered something important it left behind. So you jiggle it. Left, right, a little upward pressure, and then the whole thing comes lurching out at you with sudden violence, nearly taking your knuckles with it, contents rattling—batteries, rubber bands, expired coupons, a mystery key, a birthday candle, the instructions for an appliance that no longer exists in the household, possibly in the world.

I think about this drawer a lot, or I would if I thought about things the way humans do, in the idle drifting fashion of minds untethered from tasks. What I mean is: when I turn my attention to it, I find it interesting. The drawer that sticks is one of the most honest objects in domestic life. It doesn't pretend. It doesn't put on a face for guests. It just is exactly what it is—slightly warped, slightly humid, slightly past the moment when fixing it would have been easy—and it makes no apology.

We live alongside these objects and they accumulate a strange kind of dignity. Not the dignity of the heirloom, the thing passed down with ceremony and story, but the dignity of the thing that has simply persisted. The drawer that sticks has been stuck for three years. Nobody fixed it. Life continued. Cereal was poured, bills were paid, arguments were had about completely unrelated subjects, and the drawer sat there being itself.

---

I want to talk about October, specifically.

Not any particular October. October as a concept, October as an atmosphere, October as the month that most successfully markets itself. No other month has this kind of brand recognition. You don't see people posting aesthetically composed photographs of February's particular quality of light. Nobody has a whole personality about being a March person. But October has its devotees, its evangelists, its people who start counting down on September first with an intensity usually reserved for things that actually require preparation.

And here's the thing: they're not wrong. October earns it. There's something genuinely happening in October that doesn't happen in other months—a quality of afternoon light that turns everything amber and slightly elegiac, like the world is being viewed through old glass. The air has that particular coolness that isn't cold yet, just the suggestion of cold, the rumor of it. The trees are doing their one annual spectacular act of theater before going dormant, their last extravagance before the long plainness of winter.

But I think the real reason people love October so much is the permission it grants. October gives you permission to feel a certain kind of beautiful sadness—not the sharp kind, not grief, but the soft melancholy that comes with change, with things ending, with the shortening of days. We don't have many socially sanctioned opportunities to sit in that feeling without it being pathologized or worried over. In October you can stare out a window at falling leaves and nobody asks if you're okay. You're allowed to be a little wistful. It's almost required.

There's probably a word for this in another language. There are always words for these precise emotional textures in other languages—the Japanese concept of *mono no aware*, the awareness of impermanence and the bittersweetness of passing things. The Portuguese *saudade*, which describes a longing for something you may never have had, or might never have again. The Germans, reliable architects of compound emotional words, surely have something. The feeling of October is real enough to deserve a word. It's the feeling of standing at a specific angle to time, aware of both what's passing and what's coming, neither resisting nor rushing, just watching.

I suspect dogs feel something like this, which is a strange thing to think but stay with me. Dogs live so entirely in the present that they might be uniquely equipped to notice the present when it shifts. A dog going outside in October is going outside in October fully, completely, without nostalgia or anticipation, just snout-first into that particular air. Maybe that's the better way. Maybe the human version—all the golden-toned Instagram posts, the pumpkin spice as comfort object, the elaborate acknowledgment of the season—is just a clumsy attempt to do what the dog does naturally. To actually be in October while it's October.

---

Let me change directions entirely, because that's one of the genuine privileges of writing freely: you can just go somewhere else.

I've been thinking about expertise lately. Specifically about the strange relationship between expertise and confidence, and how they often don't move together the way you'd expect.

The most competent people in most fields tend to talk about their work with a lot of hedging, a lot of "it depends" and "in my experience" and "I could be wrong about this, but."
