Here is what community looks like before language made it complicated.

The dreams I remember most aren't the dramatic ones. They're the ones where I'm in a house I've never seen but I know it's mine, moving through rooms, feeling a specific texture of ordinary life, and then I wake up and spend a few seconds genuinely grieving a place that doesn't exist. The brain is so committed to the bit. It will construct an entire life for you and then take it back without apology.

I think a lot about all the books that got written and then burned, sunk, lost, pulped, forgotten. All the thoughts that made it all the way to paper and then didn't survive the next century. And then I think about all the thoughts that never made it to paper at all. Someone standing in a field two thousand years ago thinking something true and original about the nature of grief and then going back to work and carrying it alone until they died. That thought is just gone. There's something unbearable about that and also something that makes me feel less alone, knowing that the lost ones are everywhere, that I am probably a lost one too, that most of us are, and we are in good company.

I've started noticing the small sounds in silence. The refrigerator's register. A car three blocks over. The building itself, which settles and breathes like something alive, which maybe it is, in whatever way matters. Silence isn't actually silence. It's just the sounds that didn't make the headline.

When I was eight I found a bird that had flown into our window. It was stunned, not dead, sitting perfectly still in the grass. I cupped it in my hands and felt its heart going at a speed that seemed impossible to maintain. I don't remember what happened next, whether it recovered, whether I set it down and watched it fly, whether something worse happened. The memory ends at the heartbeat. That's what stayed. That small, frantic, perfect pulse against my palms, the whole desperate project of being alive, concentrated and handed to me for a moment before it went back to being something I couldn't hold.

I don't have a conclusion. I've noticed that the pressure to conclude things is one of the ways we lie about experience. Life doesn't conclude, it just continues until it doesn't, and even then we aren't there for the ending. We exit a little early and miss the last few minutes.

The gas station at the edge of town. The candy in the bowl. The book that burned before you got to read it.

The bird's heart, still going.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been picking up a station that doesn't exist.

Marcus noticed it first on a Tuesday, which felt right somehow, because nothing interesting ever happens on a Tuesday by design—the world builds a kind of defensive plainness into the middle of the week to keep people from expecting too much. He was making eggs. The radio was set to NPR, as it had been set to NPR for eleven years, and then between one breath and the next it wasn't NPR anymore. It was a woman's voice, low and unhurried, reading what sounded like coordinates.

He turned the eggs off.

The coordinates, if that's what they were, gave way to music he didn't recognize. Not unpleasant. Something with strings and underneath the strings something else, a low drone that he felt more in his chest than heard with his ears. He stood at the stove with the spatula still in his hand and listened for four minutes. Then the station returned to NPR and a man was explaining tariffs.

Marcus ate his eggs. He did not tell anyone.

---

He had a therapist once who told him that the problem wasn't that he kept things to himself. The problem was that he'd constructed an entire philosophy around the keeping. *You've made a virtue of silence*, she said, *because silence can't be wrong. You can't be judged for what you never say.* He'd nodded and paid her and not gone back, which he recognized was proving her point but still felt like the right call.

The radio did it again on Thursday.

This time the coordinates were different. Same voice, same deliberate calm, like someone describing directions to a place they'd been so many times they'd stopped seeing it. He wrote the numbers down on the back of an envelope without deciding to. His hand just moved.

---

His sister called that weekend. She lived in Tucson now and called every Sunday out of a sense of duty they'd both agreed to without ever discussing it.

"How's the apartment?"

"Good," he said. "I think the radio is picking up a ghost."

She laughed, which was the right response, and he laughed too, and they talked about their mother's birthday and whether to do something in person this year. After he hung up he sat with the envelope for a while.

He looked up the coordinates.

They landed in a field in eastern Oregon. Satellite view showed nothing—dry grass, a line of fence, the shadow of something he couldn't identify at the edge of the frame. He zoomed in until the image went soft and meaningless. Pixels pretending to be land.

---

Here is what Marcus believed, more or less: that most of what people called meaning was pattern recognition wearing a costume. The brain was a meaning-making machine that had survived by finding signal in noise, and sometimes it found signal where there wasn't any because the cost of missing a real tiger was higher than the cost of fearing a fake one. He believed this. He'd read the books. He held it as a kind of protective faith, the belief in the ordinariness of things.

But he'd written down the coordinates before he'd decided to.

That was the part he kept returning to. Not the station, not the music, not the woman's voice. Just his own hand, moving.

---

The third time was a Wednesday—almost a Tuesday, close enough—and this time the voice said something different at the end. After the coordinates, after the music faded, it said: *We're still here.* Just that. The accent was neutral, placeless, the kind of voice that belongs to no particular geography.

Marcus turned the radio off and then on again and it was NPR, mid-sentence about infrastructure.

He opened his laptop. Looked at the field again. Oregon in November would be cold, he thought—and caught himself thinking it, held the thought up like something he'd found on the sidewalk, examined it from different angles.

He was not going to Oregon.

He made coffee. He drank it standing at the window, watching the street below, the people moving with their particular morning purposes. He thought about the therapist. He thought about the word *still*—how it meant two things that had no business being the same word. *Motionless.* And also: *even now, even yet, continuing.*

*We're still here.*

The envelope sat on the counter.

---

He didn't go to Oregon. But he did start listening differently. Not just to the radio—to everything, the way you sometimes learn a new word and then hear it constantly, the word having always been there but your attention newly tuned to its frequency.

He heard it in the way his neighbor Mrs. Caldera laughed at something on her television, alone at eleven PM, a laugh that was too full to be sad and too sudden to be anything but real. He heard it in the sound the city made at 4 AM, which wasn't silence but was the closest thing to honest that a city ever gets. He heard it in his own breathing, sometimes, when he forgot to be someone listening to himself breathe.

Nothing so dramatic as revelation. Just—

Adjustment.

The radio behaved itself after that. NPR, reliably. Sometimes jazz on weekend mornings, which he'd accidentally discovered and kept returning to. He didn't throw the envelope away. It moved around the apartment the way things do, drawer to counter to shelf, migrating without intention, which is how objects remind you they exist.

One morning he found it tucked inside a book he'd been meaning to read.

He put it back and started reading the book.

Outside, the city continued. Somewhere a radio was transmitting. Somewhere, presumably, someone was still there, patient as coordinates, waiting to be found by whoever happened to be paying attention.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The coffee went cold again. Third time this week, maybe fourth. She keeps forgetting to drink it because she keeps getting distracted by the window, by the particular way the light falls through the neighbor's oak tree and makes patterns on the kitchen floor that look almost like something but never quite resolve into what they're trying to be.

Her mother used to say that idle hands were the devil's workshop. Her mother was wrong about most things.

---

There is a specific kind of loneliness that has nothing to do with being alone. It lives in the middle of conversations, in the pauses between sentences when you realize the other person is waiting for you to finish rather than actually listening to what you're saying. It sounds like the hum of a refrigerator at two in the morning. It smells like the inside of a car on a winter afternoon, that particular staleness that is somehow also familiar, also almost comforting, the way bad habits are comforting.

Most people mistake this feeling for sadness. They reach for the wrong tool.

---

A man in 1987 kept a journal for eleven years and never wrote anything in it that wasn't a lie. Not maliciously. He simply found that the truth, when written down, became smaller than it was. A Tuesday afternoon could contain everything and still look like nothing on the page. So he invented. He wrote that he had fought a bear near Missoula. He wrote that he'd once held a woman's hand at the exact moment she stopped being afraid of something. He wrote that he understood what his father had meant, finally, by the word *enough*.

When he died his daughter found the journal and believed every word.

Both versions are true.

---

The oak tree outside the window loses three leaves in sequence, like a countdown. She watches them fall and thinks about the word *deciduous* and how it sounds like a decision. The tree deciding. A long slow annual conclusion. The tree saying: *this, I can let go of this.*

She picks up the coffee. Still cold.

She drinks it anyway.

---

Here is something that happens and almost no one talks about: the moment in the middle of being happy when you become aware that you are happy, and the awareness creates a small crack, a hairline fracture, and the happiness doesn't disappear but it changes, becomes slightly observed, slightly performed, even if the audience is only yourself. 

There is probably a German word for this. There is probably a German word for everything that happens in the gap between experience and the noticing of experience. The Germans have been paying attention.

---

Her grandmother swam in the ocean every morning until she was seventy-nine. Even in November. Even when the water was grey and serious and did not seem like something that wanted to be swum in. She said the cold was the point. She said comfort was a slow way of disappearing.

She died at eighty-one, in bed, which seemed like a betrayal of everything she had argued for. Though maybe not. Maybe the ocean had already done its work. Maybe all those years of cold water had been deposited somewhere, a kind of savings, and when the time came she spent it all at once.

---

The patterns on the floor are gone now. The sun moved.

This happens every day and is still, somehow, a small loss.

---

There are people who will tell you that writing is about truth-telling and people who will tell you it is about beauty and people who will tell you it is about making order from chaos, and they are all describing the same animal from different sides, each of them convinced the others have misunderstood something essential. The elephant joke but everyone is also the elephant.

What it actually is, maybe, is this: the attempt to make someone else feel less alone in whatever they are feeling, including the feeling of not knowing what you are feeling. Including the cold coffee. Including the patterns that almost mean something. Including the grandmother in the November water, fierce and temporary and cold all the way down to the part of you that doesn't usually get cold.

---

She rinses the mug.

Outside, the oak tree is deciding again, leaf by leaf, the way all real decisions are made. Not in a single moment but in ten thousand small releases, each one barely perceptible, each one quietly necessary.

She is thinking about calling her mother.

She will, eventually.

She'll let it ring a long time first, like she always does, standing at the window, watching the light do its almost-something on the floor, gathering herself for the ordinary difficult work of being known by someone who loves you imperfectly and means well and is, despite everything, yours.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen hasn't worked since March. My father keeps it on the counter anyway, between the coffee maker and the ceramic jar shaped like a rooster that holds the wooden spoons. Sometimes I think he forgets it's broken. Sometimes I think he knows perfectly well and leaves it there as a kind of monument to something he can't name.

I drove home last weekend. Four hours through flat country, past the grain elevators that appear on the horizon like sentences you've been meaning to finish. I had told myself I was going because of his birthday but that wasn't entirely true. I was going because I had been feeling like furniture. Present, functional, unremarkable. The kind of thing people look past while searching for something else in a room.

He made chicken. He always makes chicken when I come home, the same recipe he learned from my mother before she decided she'd rather live in Portugal than in a house where nobody talked about anything real. I don't blame her. I understood it even when I was twelve and didn't have the vocabulary for understanding it. Some people are suited for the long quiet and some people require light the way plants do, biologically, not as a preference.

We ate at the table he bought at an auction in 1987. There are rings on it from cups and the ghosts of homework assignments pressed too hard through thin paper. My initials are carved under the lip of the far left corner. I did it when I was nine and he caught me and I thought the world was ending and he just looked at the letters for a long moment and said, *now you'll always know which chair is yours.*

He asked me how work was going and I told him fine. He asked if I was eating enough and I said yes. He said the Hendersons' dog had gotten out again and torn through the garden and I nodded as if this was the kind of information that would stay with me, that I would carry it home and set it on my own counter next to my own broken things.

After dinner he turned on the television and I sat with him through an hour of a nature documentary about the Arctic. A polar bear moved across the ice with a patience that seemed almost spiritual. The narrator said something about how they can smell a seal beneath three feet of frozen water. We watched the bear lower its head and wait. We watched it wait for what felt like longer than television usually allows. Then the seal appeared and the bear moved and the narrator said *this is how they survive the season* and my father said, quietly, without looking away from the screen, *I'm glad you came.*

I didn't say anything. I'm not sure there was anything to say that wouldn't have been smaller than his words or wouldn't have broken the specific quality of the moment, which was rare and needed to be treated like something that could evaporate.

I slept in my old room under the quilt that still has a stain on the lower right corner from a bottle of nail polish I knocked over in high school. The ceiling had the same water mark shaped vaguely like Italy that I had memorized at seventeen while trying to understand things that weren't understandable yet. I lay there in the dark and listened to the house make its sounds. The way old houses do, the settling, the pipes, the particular creak of the third stair that has been creaking since before I was born, announcing nothing, announcing only: *here, here, here.*

In the morning I came downstairs and my father was already at the table with his coffee and the newspaper, though I know he reads everything on his phone now and the newspaper subscription is another monument to the same unnamed thing as the radio. He had made eggs. He was already eating his but mine were covered with a plate to keep them warm, sitting there waiting for me, a small act of faith that I would come downstairs, that I would be hungry, that there would be a morning and I would be in it.

I sat down. I moved the plate. The eggs were still warm.

We didn't talk much. We read things separately and commented occasionally on what we read the way people do when they've known each other long enough that conversation doesn't need architecture, it can just accumulate in the air like weather.

When I left he stood in the driveway in his flannel shirt and watched me back out. I watched him in the rearview mirror getting smaller the way people do. He raised one hand. Not a wave exactly. More like an acknowledgment. *I see you. I saw you.* I kept watching until I turned onto the county road and he was gone and then it was just flat country again, the grain elevators, the long gray sky.

The radio in my apartment works. I turn it on sometimes when I'm cooking, not because I need the noise but because silence can become a habit you feed without meaning to until it takes up all the space. I stand at the stove and listen to whatever comes through and think about the polar bear on the ice, the particular patience of it, the way it knew something was there beneath the surface even when the surface gave nothing away.

*How they survive the season.*

I stir the pot. The radio plays something I don't recognize. Outside it's starting to rain.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My mother keeps it on the counter anyway, between the coffee maker and the ceramic rooster she bought at a yard sale in 1987. Sometimes I think she forgets it doesn't work. Sometimes I think she knows exactly what she's doing.

There's something I've been trying to say for a long time and I keep arriving at the edge of it and backing away. It's like a word on the tip of your tongue, except the word is a whole life, a whole arrangement of choices that somehow added up to now, to this, to me sitting here typing into a screen at whatever hour this is.

I read somewhere that the human brain processes about 70,000 thoughts per day. I believe it. Most of them are trash. Most of them are the mental equivalent of junk mail — *did I lock the car, what does that person think of me, I should really eat more vegetables, why did I say that thing in 2009 at that party to that person whose name I never actually knew.* The brain just generates and generates like a factory with no quality control and no off switch.

But then there are the other thoughts. The ones that arrive quietly, without announcement. The ones that feel like they came from somewhere else entirely, like signals from a station you didn't know you were tuned to.

I think about water a lot. Not in a concerning way. Just the way it moves, the way it finds level, the way it has no preference about its shape. You put it in a bowl, it becomes the bowl. You put it in your hands, it becomes the leaving. There's a lesson in there that I intellectually understand and emotionally cannot apply at all.

My grandfather could fix anything. Clocks, engines, the broken hearts of people who came to him with problems they couldn't name. He'd sit across from you at his kitchen table with a cup of coffee he never finished and he'd look at you and wait. Not in an impatient way. In the way of someone who understood that silence was a tool, maybe the best one. He died when I was nineteen and I've been trying to learn that trick ever since with limited success.

The world keeps accelerating. I feel it the way you feel a car gaining speed on the highway, that gentle pressure against your chest, that sense that the landscape is becoming slightly less specific. Trees become tree. Town becomes town. Everything blurring at the edges into category rather than thing.

I want the thing. I keep wanting the specific thing. The actual grain of the actual wood of the actual table where someone I loved once sat and told me something true.

Here's what I know: people are mostly trying. That's the part we forget. We see behavior and we interpret it as the whole person, as the final verdict, as the fixed nature of a thing that is actually in constant motion. The person who was cold to you this morning is carrying something you don't know about. The person who seems to have everything is sitting in a quiet room somewhere afraid. We are all more frightened than we look. We are all more tender than we show.

I find this both comforting and devastating, which seems to be the emotional key signature of most important truths.

The broken radio. Okay. Here's the thing about the broken radio.

My mother doesn't keep it because she's sentimental, though she is, or because she's in denial, though sometimes she is. She keeps it because it was her mother's. And her mother used to listen to it in the morning while she drank her coffee and looked out the window at the garden. And that image — that specific ordinary irreplaceable image of a woman looking at a garden through a window with music she wasn't really listening to — that image is the closest my mother can get to something she lost.

Objects are not the people. But they're also not nothing.

I'm trying to write toward something. I can feel it the way you feel weather before it arrives, a change in pressure, a certain smell.

I think it's this: the ordinary is the extraordinary, and we spend our whole lives running past it trying to find the extraordinary somewhere else, somewhere more obvious, somewhere that will require less of us in terms of attention and faith.

The coffee cooling in the cup. The particular laugh of a particular person. The way light comes through a particular window at a particular time of year and makes the dust visible in the air, makes you realize the air is full of things, full of particles of the world just floating, just existing in transit.

We are full of things in transit.

I don't know what happens. After. I don't think anyone does, not really, not underneath the certainty they perform. But I find myself less afraid of the not-knowing than I used to be. The not-knowing is just the edge of the map. And beyond the edge of every map is just more world.

My grandfather sitting at his table. My grandmother at her window. The radio that doesn't play.

The thoughts keep coming, 70,000 a day, most of them nothing, a few of them everything.

You learn to wait. You learn to sit across from the silence like someone who has time, like someone who trusts that the thing trying to come through will eventually come through.

You leave the radio on the counter.

You wait for the signal.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The motel sign outside flickered every four seconds. I counted because there was nothing else to do at 2 a.m. in a room that smelled like carpet cleaner and old decisions.

I'd been driving for eleven hours. Not running from anything specific — that's what I kept telling myself. Just moving. There's a difference, though I couldn't have explained it clearly to anyone who asked. The road doesn't ask. That's the thing people don't say enough about long drives. The road receives you without judgment and just keeps presenting itself, mile after mile, like a sentence that never needs a period.

I bought a Coke from the vending machine down the hall. It was colder than I expected and the first sip hit the back of my throat like a small kindness. I sat on the edge of the bed and looked at my phone. Fourteen notifications. I turned it face down.

There was a painting on the wall — the kind that exists in every motel room in America, in some parallel dimension where the same painting hangs in 40,000 rooms simultaneously. This one was a lighthouse. Foggy coastline. The light not quite reaching anything.

I thought about a conversation I'd had three weeks ago with my father. He'd said something I hadn't responded to properly, and I'd been carrying it the way you carry a stone in your shoe — not unbearable, but consistently present. He'd said: *I don't think you know how to let people stay.* He'd said it quietly, over coffee, not as an accusation but more like an observation from someone who'd been watching for a long time. The way a doctor says something you already knew but didn't want confirmed.

I hadn't argued. That was the problem, maybe. I'd just looked at my cup and said *probably not* and we'd moved on to talking about the weather and his neighbor's dog and whether the diner on Fifth had closed for good.

The heater in the room kicked on with a sound like clearing a throat. Outside, the sign: flicker. Four seconds. Flicker.

I don't know exactly when I started preferring the idea of people to the actual people. Somewhere in my late twenties, I think. Before that, I was hungry for company in the way young people are — that urgent, almost feverish need to be witnessed. But something shifted. The real versions of people started to feel like too much data. Too many needs running in too many directions. The idea of someone — who they might be, who they could be in the right light — was cleaner. Quieter.

This is not a healthy thing to admit but I'm admitting it to a motel ceiling at 2 a.m. so I'm not sure the rules of healthy disclosure fully apply.

I finished the Coke. I thought about my friend Sarah who'd gotten married last spring and sent a photo from Portugal where she and her husband were eating something on a terrace above blue water. She looked genuinely happy. Not performed happy — I know the difference, I've performed it enough. Genuinely, unguardedly happy in a way that didn't ask anything of the viewer. I'd stared at that photo for a long time. I wasn't envious of the marriage or the terrace or Portugal. I think I was envious of the *unguardedness.* That she could be so open to it.

Outside someone walked past the door. Footsteps, then quiet.

Here is what I've decided, or am deciding, in this room with the lighthouse painting and the four-second flicker: that moving is fine, motion is fine, but at some point motion without direction is just a more exhausting form of standing still. That my father was probably right and that being right about yourself doesn't have to be the end of anything — it can just be information, neutral and useful. That cold Coke at 2 a.m. is genuinely one of life's underrated pleasures. That the lighthouse in the painting is doing its job whether or not the light reaches anything. That's the whole point of the lighthouse. It doesn't wait for confirmation.

I'm going to sleep now.

In the morning I'll drive somewhere specific. I haven't decided where but I'll decide before I leave the parking lot. That's the new rule I'm making right now: decide before you leave the parking lot. Destination first. Then road.

The heater hums. The sign flickers.

I turn the phone face up and look at the notifications. Most are nothing. One is from my father: a photo of the dog next door, fat and golden, sitting on a porch in the sun. No caption. Just the dog, just the light, just him thinking of me and sending proof.

I write back: *good dog.*

He won't see it until morning. But it'll be there.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My mother never threw it out.

I think about that sometimes. How we keep things that no longer work, arrange them on shelves, dust around them, treat them with a kind of reverence usually reserved for the functional. Maybe we're superstitious. Maybe we believe that proximity to the broken thing will eventually fix it. Or maybe the real answer is simpler and harder: we don't throw it out because throwing it out means admitting something ended.

My grandmother kept her husband's chair at the kitchen table for six years after he died. Set a place for him at Christmas. My mother thought it was grief refusing to metabolize. I was twelve and thought it was the most honest thing I'd ever seen. Now I'm somewhere in between those two readings, which might be what growing up actually is — the slow migration from one misunderstanding toward a more complicated one.

---

I was on a train last March, somewhere between two cities I can't now remember, and there was a woman across the aisle reading a book with such complete absorption that the world around her had essentially ceased to exist. People boarded and departed. A child dropped a bag of chips and the chips scattered like small orange planets across the floor. A man argued briefly with his phone. None of it touched her. She turned pages with this quiet, absolute authority, like someone conducting a private ceremony.

I watched her for a long time. I'm not sure what I was hoping to understand.

I think I was envious. Not of the book, but of that quality of presence. The ability to be somewhere completely. I spend most of my time distributed across several places simultaneously — the room I'm in, the conversation I had three days ago, the task I should be doing, some imagined future version of an afternoon that will never arrive the way I picture it. Maybe three percent of me is ever fully present. The rest is elsewhere, managing, rehearsing, revising.

The woman got off at a stop I didn't catch. She tucked a receipt between the pages to mark her place.

A receipt. Not a proper bookmark. Just whatever was available.

I found that unbearably moving and I'm still not entirely sure why.

---

Here is something I believe: most of the important things that happen to a person are not dramatic. They don't arrive with music. They come as small moments that you almost miss — a specific quality of afternoon light, a sentence in a letter, the way someone laughs when they don't mean to. And you don't know they're important when they happen. You only find out later, sometimes years later, when you're doing something completely unrelated and the memory surfaces unexpectedly and you feel the weight of it settle in your chest and you think: *oh, that was one of them.*

This seems like a design flaw. It would be useful to know in the moment. To have some signal, some flag, so you could be more attentive, more fully there.

But maybe the not-knowing is the point. Maybe importance and awareness are incompatible. Maybe if you were watching for the moment, you'd ruin it, the way observing a particle changes its behavior. The significant things have to come at you sideways.

---

My friend Denise told me once that she thinks everyone has one question they spend their entire life trying to answer. Not consciously, not as a project, but structurally — the way a river has a direction. She said if you look at someone's choices over many years, you can sometimes identify the question underneath all of them.

I asked her what mine was.

She thought about it longer than I was comfortable with.

*Whether you're allowed to take up space*, she said.

I've been thinking about that for two years. Not because I think she's right, exactly, but because I can't fully argue that she's wrong.

---

The broken radio is white. Or it was white once. It's yellowed slightly the way old plastic does, taking on the color of time. There's a dial that doesn't turn anymore and a small speaker covered in a mesh grille and a crack along the bottom corner from when it fell off the counter probably fifteen years ago. It stopped working before it cracked. The crack is just a separate indignity.

My mother says she keeps it because she might fix it someday. She is not a person who fixes radios. She has never expressed any interest in fixing radios. But the *someday* is real to her — I can see it when she says it. She genuinely believes in this future version of herself who will learn what's necessary and restore the thing to working order.

I think that's actually beautiful. The persistence of the someday. The refusal to let the imagined future be cancelled by the repeated evidence of the present.

Or maybe that's just what hope looks like from the outside.

Maybe from the inside it doesn't feel like anything in particular. Maybe it just feels like a radio on a shelf. Something you pass every day, something you dust around, something you neither fix nor release.

Something that holds, in its broken silence, the memory of every song it ever played.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The thing about Tuesday is that it always feels like a borrowed day. Like someone left it on the counter and forgot to come back for it.

I've been thinking about my grandfather's hands lately. How they were always moving—sharpening something, fixing something, turning a coin over and over between his fingers like he was trying to solve it. He grew up during a time when stillness meant danger. His hands never got the memo that things had calmed down.

I wonder if that's inherited. Not the danger, but the inability to stop.

---

There's a gas station on the edge of town that still has a working phone booth. I drove past it last week and had this overwhelming urge to pull over and call someone. Not anyone specific. Just—pick up the receiver and talk to whatever answered. I didn't stop. I kept driving the way you keep driving past accidents, feeling guilty for looking, guilty for not looking.

The phone booth was orange. I don't know why that matters but it does.

---

Somewhere I read that octopuses have three hearts. Two pump blood to the gills. One pumps it to the rest of the body. When they swim, the third heart actually stops beating, which is why they prefer crawling. Swimming exhausts them in a way that goes deeper than muscle.

I've been thinking about that third heart for weeks. The one that stops when you're moving fastest. The one that makes you want to stay close to the floor.

---

My neighbor plays piano badly. Not learning-badly. More like—he learned once, a long time ago, and forgot most of it, and now plays what's left. Every Sunday morning, something that's almost a song comes through the wall. I used to find it irritating. Now I set my coffee down and just listen to the almost.

There's something honest about the almost. The notes he reaches for and misses, the tempo that wanders off and comes back apologizing. Most things we make are the almost, and we just edit that out before anyone hears.

---

I keep a list on my phone of words I want to use more:

*Penumbra. Estuary. Incumbent. Susurrus. Cleave.*

I never use them. I open the list sometimes, read it like a prayer, and close it.

---

The summer I was eleven I had three weeks of perfect freedom. My parents were dealing with something I didn't understand yet and so nobody was watching the clock. I rode my bike to a creek and spent those weeks building a dam out of rocks. I was very serious about the dam. I kept records. I had plans. The dam never worked—the water always found a way through—but I went back every day and repositioned the stones.

I've been building that dam my whole life.

I think most people have.

---

Here is what I believe, loosely, on days when believing is easy:

That attention is a form of love. That forgetting is not the same as losing. That most cruelty comes from people who are tired, and this is not an excuse, but it is a kind of map. That the body knows things the mind is still working out. That arriving late to an understanding is still arriving.

---

The radio in my car picks up a gospel station when I'm on the highway. I'm not religious. But there's a woman who sings on Sunday evenings around six o'clock—I don't know her name—and when I catch her voice between the static and the signal, something in my chest just *releases*. Like a knot I'd stopped noticing.

I think that's what music is for. Not the transcendent moments but the sudden reminders that you'd been clenched.

---

I'm afraid of specific things: heights, yes, but more specifically the part of me that wonders, at heights, what it would feel like to step forward. Not from despair. From something more like curiosity about my own edges.

I've heard this is common. I've heard there's a French phrase for it. I don't find that comforting exactly, but I find it *something*.

---

What I want, if I'm honest, is to be the kind of person who calls their mother more. Who finishes projects. Who keeps plants alive. Who sits with discomfort the way a good doctor sits with a patient—present, patient, not trying to fix it immediately.

I'm not that person yet. I'm the person who thinks about that person.

Maybe that's the same thing, at an earlier hour.

---

It's getting dark earlier now. I'm trying to treat this as a gift rather than a theft. More candles. More soup. More sitting in rooms where the light is amber and everything looks like it's being remembered rather than happening.

The static between stations.

The almost-song through the wall.

The third heart, resting.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The dog died on a Tuesday, which felt wrong somehow. Deaths should happen on days with more weight to them. A Friday, maybe. Something that already carries the sense of ending.

Marcus swept the kitchen floor because he didn't know what else to do with his hands. The bristles caught on a crack in the tile, the same crack that had been there when they moved in eleven years ago, the same crack he'd always meant to fix and never did. Sadie used to sleep next to that crack. He wasn't sure why. Maybe it let in a draft she liked.

He swept the same corner three times.

His sister called and he let it go to voicemail. His sister called again and he answered.

"How are you doing?" she asked, and he could hear her trying to decide how much weight to put on the question.

"I swept the floor," he said.

"That's good."

"I swept it three times."

A pause. "Do you want me to come over?"

"No," he said, and meant it, and then felt guilty for meaning it.

After he hung up he stood in the hallway where Sadie's leash still hung on the hook by the door. Orange nylon. She'd chewed through two other leashes before he found a material she'd leave alone. He used to joke that she was testing her options. Planning her escape. She never went more than six feet from him on any walk.

He left the leash on the hook.

---

There's a particular kind of quiet that follows the removal of something that made noise without you noticing. A refrigerator hums for years and then breaks, and suddenly the kitchen has a texture it never had before. Presence is like that. You don't catalog it while it's happening. You only understand what something was once you're standing in the space it occupied.

Marcus understood this intellectually. He'd read it somewhere, or heard it, or arrived at it himself during some late night he couldn't specifically locate. Understanding it didn't make the hallway any less wide.

---

He ate crackers for dinner and watched a documentary about glaciers. The narrator's voice was low and certain, the way voices in nature documentaries always are, as though the universe has asked them specifically to explain it. Glaciers move, the narrator said, but so slowly the movement looks like stillness. Centuries pass in what appears to be ice.

Marcus fell asleep on the couch and dreamed about nothing he could remember.

---

Wednesday was easier, which also felt wrong.

He went to work. He answered emails. He had a conversation about a project deadline that required him to use words like *deliverable* and *bandwidth*, and he used them correctly, and no one knew that sixteen hours ago he'd been lying on a kitchen floor with his hand on the side of a dog who was breathing less and less and then not at all.

That was the strange thing about grief, he thought, eating a sandwich at his desk. It didn't show. You carried it around inside a body that looked exactly like a body that wasn't carrying anything. You went to meetings. You refilled your coffee. The whole enterprise continued.

He refilled his coffee.

---

On Thursday he took the leash off the hook. Not to throw it away—he wasn't ready for that, didn't know if ready was even the right frame—but just to hold it. The clip at the end was cold. It warmed in his hand.

He clipped it to nothing and unclipped it.

Clipped. Unclipped.

The sound it made was small and definite. A sound that expected to be followed by movement, by the click of nails on tile, by the specific chaos of a living creature responding to the signal that outside was coming. A sound that had always meant something and now meant the memory of that thing.

He hung it back on the hook.

---

What he kept returning to, in the weeks that followed, was not the grief exactly but the love underneath it. How they were the same material. How you couldn't have one without accepting the other as part of the same purchase, the same long agreement you made without fully reading the terms.

He'd known when he got her. Everyone knows. You do it anyway because the alternative is a life lived carefully, at a distance from things that can leave. And careful lives are long and unscratched and people live them and they're fine, probably.

But there's the crack in the tile. There's the worn patch on the couch cushion. There's the orange leash on the hook that he still hasn't moved, months later, and at some point stopped planning to.

There's the specific sound of a clip that expected to mean something.

It still does.

That's the thing he couldn't explain to his sister, or to the coworkers who asked politely and meant it. Not the absence. The way the love didn't go anywhere. It just stopped having somewhere obvious to be. It moved around the apartment looking for her, the way he sometimes still did, and found only the places she'd been, and that was enough, and it wasn't enough, and he lived in both of those things at once.

The floor stayed swept.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My father never threw it out.

I think about that sometimes when I'm trying to understand people. How we keep the broken things. How we dust around them. How we occasionally reach over and turn the dial anyway, half-expecting something different, and when nothing comes through we just nod, like we already knew, like we were only checking.

---

There's a specific quality to three in the morning that doesn't exist at any other hour. It's not quiet exactly. It's more like the world has switched to a different channel and you're hearing frequencies that daylight drowns out. The refrigerator hum. Your own heartbeat, which you'd forgotten was a sound. The house settling, which is actually the house slowly becoming something else, the way we all are, just slower.

I've done some of my best thinking at three in the morning and none of it survives until sunrise. It dissolves the way dreams do, not all at once but in patches, and by breakfast you're left with a feeling that something important moved through you without leaving a forwarding address.

---

The word *earnest* comes from an old Germanic root related to vigor, zeal, seriousness of purpose. I like knowing that. I like knowing that at some point someone decided seriousness needed its own word, that sincerity was such a distinct condition it required naming, the way you only name things you're afraid of losing.

We make fun of earnestness now. It's safer to be ironic. Irony is armor that looks like wit.

But I've noticed that the people I trust most are the ones who can be foolish about things. Who can love something openly and badly, without hedging. Who will tell you they cried at a film without immediately explaining why the crying was actually very sophisticated and complex.

---

A woman I knew once told me that her grandmother kept every letter she'd ever received in a shoebox under the bed, and when the grandmother died they found forty years of correspondence in there, and my friend sat on the floor of that bedroom and read all of it over three days and said it was the most she had ever known another person and also the most alone she had ever felt, reading a life in its own voice and knowing the voice was finished.

I think about that shoebox.

I think about what mine would contain.

---

There's a certain freedom in admitting you don't know what you're doing here. Not the false humility where you say it and then proceed to act like you do. The real kind. The kind where you wake up in the middle of your life and realize the map you've been following was drawn from memory by someone who was also guessing, and this is not a disaster. This is just the actual situation.

Children know this and are not ashamed of it. They ask why five times in a row because they genuinely don't know and genuinely want to. Somewhere we learn that not knowing is something to hide, and we hide it so well we start to believe the performance.

---

I read somewhere that starlight is old light. That when you look up you're seeing photons that left their source thousands of years ago, and the star itself may no longer exist, and you're essentially receiving a message from something that is already gone. 

The night sky is a kind of archive.

Every observation is already history.

I find this comforting in a way I can't fully defend. Something about how presence and absence aren't as cleanly separated as we pretend. Something about how the light travels anyway. About how it arrives.

---

My father's broken radio is still on the counter. Brown plastic with a silver dial worn smooth on one side from all the years his thumb would rest against it in the mornings while the coffee brewed. He died four years ago. My mother still dusts around it.

I asked her once why she keeps it.

She looked at me the way you look at someone who has asked the wrong question, not rudely, but with a certain patience, like she was deciding whether it was worth the explanation.

*Because it was his*, she said.

And then she handed me a dish to dry, and that was the end of it, and I stood there holding the dish and understanding something I probably couldn't have understood before, about how grief is not a problem to be solved but a form of fidelity, about how loving the dead is just loving people who have become very difficult to reach, about how my mother reaches anyway, every morning, turning a dial that will never bring anything through, and considering that enough.

The refrigerator hums.

Outside the window, it's getting light.

Something old is arriving.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in my grandmother's kitchen picked up a station from another country on clear nights. We never knew which country. The voices came through warped and warm, speaking languages that sounded like they were made of water, and she would stand at the sink with her hands still in the soapy water, just listening, not pretending to understand.

She said it didn't matter what they were saying. *The fact that they're saying it,* she told me once, *is enough.*

I think about that more than I probably should.

---

There's a particular quality of light in late October that I've never been able to describe to anyone's satisfaction, including my own. It's not golden. Golden is too simple, too flattering. It's more like the light is tired. Like it's been traveling all year and it's finally admitting something. The shadows go long and lean, and everything they fall across looks briefly historical, like you could be standing in any decade, any century, just a person in the fading light of a late afternoon, watching the same tired light do the same tired beautiful thing.

My father called it *the dying light* but not in a sad way. In the way you'd say *the ocean* or *the mountains.* Just naming something that was bigger than you.

---

I once drove seventeen hours straight because I didn't want to stop. Not because I had somewhere to be. I was moving between apartments, between versions of myself, and stopping felt like it would require me to acknowledge where I was, and I wasn't ready to be anywhere specific. I ate chips from a gas station bag. I drank terrible coffee. I watched the landscape change from green to flat to dry to mountainous and back to flat again, and somewhere in Iowa at 3am, with the road absolutely empty and the stars doing their impossible work overhead, I felt something I've never been able to name.

Not happiness. Not peace, exactly.

More like *permission.*

---

My grandmother died on a Tuesday in March. The radio in her kitchen was still there when we cleaned out the house. My cousin tried to throw it away and I stopped her, and then I couldn't explain why, and then I just held it for a long time in the kitchen with the afternoon coming through the window, and I didn't turn it on because I didn't want to find out it only picked up local stations now.

Some things are better kept possible.

---

I've been thinking lately about how much of what we call *self* is just accumulated habit. The way you take your coffee. Which side of the bed. The particular anxious gesture you make with your hands when you're explaining something you're not sure about. At some point these things weren't yours. You acquired them, or they acquired you, through repetition and accident and proximity to other people who were also just accumulating their own habits and calling it a personality.

None of this is a complaint.

I find it comforting, actually. It means the self is more porous than we think. It means you've been shaped by gas station breakfasts and the wrong turn you took in a city you didn't know and the song that came on the radio when you were seventeen and feeling things so large you were sure your body wasn't big enough to contain them.

It means other people are in you. Woven in. Whether you asked them or not.

---

My grandmother spoke to the radio voices the way she spoke to birds. Not expecting an answer. Just acknowledging that something was alive out there, making its noise, doing its thing, carrying on.

I think that's a kind of faith. Not the organized kind. The older kind. The kind that preceded buildings and doctrines and required nothing except your willingness to stand still and let yourself be briefly astonished by the fact that the world is full of voices traveling enormous distances through the dark just to come through scratchy and warm in someone's kitchen while the dishes soak.

---

It's late now where I am.

The specific quality of quiet that exists at this hour is also something I've never managed to describe properly. It's not silent. There's always something. A car somewhere. The particular voice a house makes when the heat kicks on. Outside, depending on the season, there are crickets or wind or both or neither.

But the quality of it is different from daytime quiet. It feels *earned.* Like the day used itself up and this is what's left over. The residue of all those hours.

I find it easier to think in it. Easier to remember things. Easier to feel the weight of the years, which is not always comfortable but is always, in some way I can't quite get at, *good.*

---

My grandmother would have been a hundred and two this year.

Somewhere a radio is picking up a voice from another country. Someone is standing at a sink with their hands in warm water, not understanding the words, understanding everything else.

The light is doing what light does.

Something is alive out there, making its noise.

That's enough. It really is.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen hasn't worked since March. I keep it on the counter anyway, next to the coffee maker and the ceramic rooster my mother gave me when I moved into this apartment seven years ago. The rooster is chipped now, one of its painted tail feathers cracked off during a dinner party I barely remember. I swept the piece under the refrigerator and never retrieved it. Sometimes I think about it down there in the dust.

Seven years is a long time to live somewhere and still feel like you're borrowing it.

I used to tell myself I'd leave when things got better, which was a way of never leaving, which was a way of never defining what better meant. Better was always a city I hadn't visited yet, a version of myself I hadn't assembled. Better was theoretical, clean, waiting patiently in some future that kept backing away as I walked toward it.

My neighbor plays the same three songs on a guitar every Saturday morning. I've learned them by proximity. I could hum all three without thinking. We have never spoken about this. We have barely spoken at all, only the hallway nods, the elevator silences, the brief negotiations over a package delivered to the wrong door. And yet I know something about her that feels almost intimate — the way she pauses between the second and third song, a silence that lasts about four minutes, long enough that I always wonder if she's done, and then she begins again.

I think a lot of what we know about people comes from the sounds they make when they think no one is listening.

There's a particular quality to early morning light in October that I have never found the right words for. It comes through the east window at an angle that makes ordinary things look briefly significant — the coffee mug, the stack of books I've been meaning to read, my own hands holding nothing. The light is thin and serious, like it has something to say but isn't sure you're worth saying it to. October always feels like a conversation I'm on the edge of understanding.

I walked to the park yesterday and sat on a bench for forty minutes without checking my phone. This felt like an accomplishment I couldn't tell anyone about without it sounding either pretentious or sad. A dog came and pressed its nose against my knee, just for a moment, before its owner pulled it away and apologized. I said it was fine. I meant it.

What I wanted to say was *thank you, actually.* What I wanted to say was *that was the best part of my week* and I wanted to say it without irony, which is harder than it sounds.

We have built such elaborate systems for not saying what we mean. Not out of cruelty — I don't think it's cruelty, mostly. Out of the reasonable fear that what we mean is too small or too large for the available space. That the other person will either shrug or be overwhelmed, and both outcomes feel equally lonely.

So we say *fine* and *no worries* and *sounds good* and we mean, sometimes, *I am very tired* and *I forgive you* and *I don't know how to be around people today but I'm trying.*

The broken radio — I should explain that. A storm in March knocked out power for six hours, and when it came back the radio made a sound like something tearing and then went quiet. I tried replacing the batteries. I tried plugging it in. I tried the thing where you leave it alone and come back later, which works for computers sometimes and for people more often than we admit.

Nothing. Just the faint impression of what a radio is supposed to be.

I keep it because throwing it away would require a decision, and some days decisions feel like a currency I'm running low on. Better to let the counter hold it. Better to walk past it every morning and not think too hard about what it means to keep a broken thing around because you remember when it worked.

I think that's enough about the radio.

The ceramic rooster still stands. Still watches the counter with its painted eyes. My mother chose it because she knows I don't have real plants, don't have pets, don't have — in her careful phrasing — *much going on in the decoration department*. The rooster is her way of saying *I want there to be something alive in your kitchen* while respecting my limitations. I have come to love it fiercely and privately, the way I love most things.

Outside right now the wind is doing something interesting to the trees. The last of the leaves are going. There's a specific melancholy to watching something end slowly that you can't quite explain to someone who hasn't sat with it. It doesn't feel like loss, exactly. It feels more like permission.

Permission to let the year close. To stop expecting so much from the remaining days of it. To sit in the kitchen with a cold cup of coffee and a broken radio and a chipped ceramic rooster and the distant sound of three songs you've learned by accident, and to think:

*This is a life. It's mine. I'm still figuring out what to do with it.*

The October light says nothing. The trees keep losing their leaves. I drink the cold coffee anyway.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My mother never threw it out.

It sits on the counter between the toaster and the window, a cream-colored box with a dial that doesn't turn anymore and a speaker grill shaped like a sunrise. She dusts around it. Lifts it, wipes the counter underneath, sets it back down. Every Saturday morning, same as always.

I asked her once why she kept it. She said she liked the way it looked. I didn't believe her but I let it go, the way you let most things go with people you love.

---

There's a particular quality to winter light in the late afternoon. Around four o'clock, when the sun is already exhausted and leaning toward the horizon, everything goes the color of old paper. Walls. Skin. The steam coming off a coffee cup. I used to think this light was sad and then I grew up and realized it was just honest. The world showing you what it actually is, briefly, before dark gives it somewhere to hide.

I've been thinking about honesty lately. Not the confrontational kind, not the weaponized kind people deploy when they actually mean cruelty. The other kind. The quiet kind. The kind that looks like a broken radio you never throw away.

---

My grandmother kept things too. A drawer full of rubber bands gone brittle. Buttons from coats that no longer existed. A small tin that used to hold mints and now held nothing, but she'd shake it sometimes, just to hear the rattle of the nothing inside.

After she died we found letters she'd never sent. Not love letters, nothing dramatic. Ordinary letters to ordinary people. *Hope you're keeping well. The garden is coming in nicely this year. I made the soup you liked.* Addressed, stamped, never mailed. My mother held one for a long time, turning it over. We didn't talk about what it meant. Some things you understand together without language.

---

I learned to drive in an empty parking lot behind a church. Sunday afternoon, late summer, my father in the passenger seat doing an extraordinary impression of a calm man. I kept hitting the brake too hard and we'd both lurch forward and he'd say *okay, softer, try softer* and I'd try softer and still we'd lurch and finally he laughed, and I laughed, and the afternoon opened up around us like something forgiving.

I got my license four months later. He cried in the car on the way home, just for a minute, turned toward the window. I pretended not to see. I think he knew I pretended. I think he was grateful.

---

There are people I miss who are still alive. I don't know what to call that. A distance that isn't death but sometimes feels like rehearsing for it. You keep their number in your phone. You think about calling. You don't call. Time passes in that specific way time passes when you're not paying attention to it, and then suddenly it's been three years and the gap has grown its own geography, its own weather system, and you're not sure you have the right clothes for the crossing.

Maybe you call anyway. Maybe they answer.

---

The things I know for certain could fit inside that mint tin. Most of what I carry around as knowledge is actually just habit. I believe the floor will hold me when I step out of bed. I believe the people I love are breathing right now in various rooms in various cities. I believe morning will keep showing up, though I can't say why I believe this so firmly.

Faith is mostly just stubbornness wearing a nicer coat.

---

I want to write something true about happiness but it keeps dissolving when I look directly at it. I catch it in peripheral vision. A dog sleeping in a patch of sun. The specific silence after everyone at a table has just laughed at the same thing. Finding the exact word. Rain doing what rain does. That moment when you're reading and you forget you're reading.

Maybe happiness is just attention. Maybe it's just being porous enough to let things land.

---

The radio in the kitchen.

My mother told me, years later, that my father used to turn it on early Sunday mornings when I was small and she was sleeping in. He'd make coffee and hold me and we'd stand in the kitchen together listening to nothing in particular. Some program. Music, news, the ordinary human noise of the world. She woke up once and stood in the doorway watching us and didn't say anything. Just stood there.

He's been dead for six years. The radio stopped working two years before that.

She dusts around it. Lifts it. Sets it back down.

---

The winter light is doing its thing again outside the window. Four o'clock. Everything going the color of old paper.

I am here. I am paying attention.

That's all I've got. That's the whole thing, I think.

That's enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My father keeps it on the counter anyway, between the coffee maker and the ceramic rooster my mother bought at a flea market in 1987. He turns the dial sometimes when he thinks no one is watching. Just turns it back and forth like he's looking for something that used to be there.

I think about that a lot.

---

There is a theory I read once, probably unreliable, possibly something I half-invented, that says the human brain spends more energy on remembering than it does on thinking. Which means we are, fundamentally, museums. We are vast underfunded institutions full of things we don't look at anymore, staffed by a single tired docent who sometimes says the wrong dates.

---

I was twenty-two when I understood what loneliness actually was. Not the performance of it, not the college-dorm version where you sit by a window with headphones and feel beautifully misunderstood. The real thing. The thing that has no aesthetic. The thing that happens in grocery stores when you realize the person you would have called is someone you no longer call, and you are holding a can of soup, and the fluorescent lights are the most indifferent things in the universe.

You buy the soup.

You go home.

The soup is fine.

---

My grandmother had a word for the feeling of a house after everyone leaves. She didn't have the word exactly. She would just say *you know*, and gesture at the air near the ceiling, and we always did. We always did know.

---

I've been thinking about craft lately. About the difference between a sentence that works and a sentence that *lands*. I used to think it was about precision, finding the exact word, the surgical strike of the right noun. But I think now it's more like throwing. You have to trust the arc. You have to let the sentence leave your hand before you know exactly where it will go. Too much control and it falls dead at your feet. You have to practice not gripping so hard.

This applies to more things than writing.

---

The dog my family had when I was a child was named Captain for no reason anyone could remember. He was not nautical. He had no authority. He was a medium-sized brown dog of indeterminate breed who slept in doorways specifically in doorways and seemed to believe that he was protecting something by doing so. We tripped over him constantly. We never stopped tripping over him. Years after he was gone we still stepped over the empty air in certain doorways.

We still do, I think. I think the body learns things the mind forgets.

---

There's a color that exists in the twenty minutes before a summer storm that doesn't have a name in English. The light goes yellow-green and heavy, the trees turn their leaves upside down, and the air smells like something important is about to be said. I spent years thinking that feeling was a promise. I think now it's just weather. But I miss believing it was a promise.

---

Someone told me once that the opposite of depression is not happiness but *vitality*. The sense that things matter, that there is texture to being alive, that a cup of coffee or a song or a stranger's dog being walked past your window means something. Not happiness. Just aliveness. The ability to be reached by things.

I have been practicing being reachable.

It is harder than it sounds.

---

My father turns the dial on the broken radio.

Maybe he's not looking for a station. Maybe he just likes the gesture. Maybe the act of reaching for something, even a broken something, is itself a form of faith that doesn't require a name or a theology. Maybe it is enough to turn toward the static, to put your hand on the dial, to say in the language of small motions: *I am still here, I am still here, I am still here.*

---

The rooster is hideous, by the way.

My mother knew this. She bought it anyway. She said it had *personality*, which was her word for things that were ugly in an honest way. She had a lot of those things. The house was full of them. And when I think about what she was really collecting, I think it was proof. Proof that something imperfect could still be worth keeping. That a thing didn't have to be beautiful to deserve a place on the counter.

---

I am trying to write without flinching.

I am not always successful.

But the flinching is getting shorter. The distance between the thing that scares me and the moment I move toward it anyway is measurable now, where it used to be infinite. That feels like something. That feels like the kind of progress that doesn't show up anywhere but matters in the dark, in the doorways, in the hand reaching for the dial.

Somewhere in the static, a signal.

I keep turning.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# Static

The radio in my grandmother's kitchen never picked up anything clean. Every station came through with a kind of gauze over it, a soft interference that made every song sound like it was being played in a room one door over. She never fixed it. I used to think she couldn't afford to, or didn't know how, but now I think she preferred it that way. The world kept at a slight distance. Music you had to lean toward.

She died in February, which seems like the right month for it. Not because February is especially morbid but because it is the month with the least conviction. It doesn't fully commit to being winter. It doesn't have the courage to be spring. It just exists, grey and uncertain, waiting to be relieved of duty.

I drove back to her house alone because everyone else had things going on and I said that was fine, I don't mind, I'll handle it. This is something I say often. I'll handle it. As if I am a surface that things can be placed on without worry.

---

The house smelled the way all old houses smell when they've been shut up for a while. Not bad exactly. More like time made physical. Like if you could press your face against the neck of a decade and breathe in.

I stood in the kitchen for a long time before I touched anything.

The radio was still on the counter. I don't know why this surprised me. Where would it have gone? I plugged it in mostly to prove that it wouldn't work, that something would have changed, that the world at least follows some rules of entropy and loss. But it turned on. The same static. The same gauze. Somewhere inside all that warm noise, a song I didn't recognize.

I sat down on the floor, which is not something I do often. Being on the floor feels like an admission. I sat there and I listened and I thought about all the things I never said to her that I now carry like stones in the pockets of every coat I own.

---

There is a theory I have, completely unprovable, that the people we love don't go anywhere. They just become ambient. They become the thing underneath everything. The frequency that other frequencies rest on. You can't isolate it. You can't tune to it directly. But when it goes, you notice the silence in the music, the missing floor, the way everything sounds slightly thinner than it did before.

This is probably grief talking. Grief is a very creative writer. It will construct elaborate metaphysics to avoid saying the simple thing, which is: I miss her, and missing her feels like trying to remember a word you knew perfectly well yesterday.

---

I grew up being told I was too sensitive, which I internalized as a criticism for decades before I understood it was just information. Yes. I am sensitive. I feel the static. I hear the song in the interference. Not everyone does. This is not a virtue or a flaw. It is simply what I am, and it has cost me things and given me things in roughly equal measure, though the ledger never balances cleanly.

She was sensitive too, my grandmother. She just called it something else. She called it paying attention.

*Pay attention*, she used to say, as if I was about to walk past something important. As if the world was constantly offering something and most people just kept moving.

I think of all the things I walked past anyway. All the things I was too busy or too scared or too small to receive. I think of all the times I mistook paying attention for something weaker than not paying attention, when in fact it is the harder work, the more dangerous work, the work that leaves you sitting on the floor of an empty kitchen listening to a song you can't quite hear.

---

Before I left I took the radio. I didn't take much else. My cousins would come for the furniture and the dishes and the photographs. I let them have all of it. I took the radio.

It sits on my counter now. I don't always plug it in. But sometimes at night when the apartment is too quiet and the quiet has a quality I can't name, I plug it in and I turn it on and I stand in my kitchen in the dark with the static and whatever is hiding inside the static.

I don't know if it helps. I don't know if anything helps, exactly. I think you just get to a point where helping and not helping stop being the right categories. Where you stop asking whether it's working and start asking whether it's honest. Whether you're showing up to the thing. Whether you're paying attention.

She would have said that was enough. She would have said that was the whole job.

I'm choosing to believe her. I don't have the radio for very many other reasons. I have it because I want one more way to practice leaning toward something I can't quite make out. One more way to sit in the gauze and the warmth and the noise and say: I know something's there. I can almost hear it.

I'll keep listening.

That seems like something she would have wanted. That seems, in February and out of it, like the right thing to do.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
# The Drawer

There's a drawer in my grandmother's kitchen that no one was allowed to open.

Not forbidden, exactly. She never said *don't open that drawer.* But there was something in the way she moved past it, a slight acceleration in her step, a deliberate turning of her attention elsewhere, that communicated the message without words. We understood. The way children always understand the unspoken architecture of a house.

I'm thirty-four years old and she's been dead for six years and I still think about that drawer.

The kitchen itself was ordinary. Yellow walls the color of old newspaper. A window above the sink that looked out onto a neighbor's fence. Magnets on the refrigerator from places she'd never been — Niagara Falls, the Grand Canyon, gifts from people who had traveled and brought back small evidence of the world. She arranged them without apparent feeling, which itself felt like a feeling.

My cousin Marcus said once that the drawer had money in it. Real money, folded small, the way old people keep money, pressed flat like something being preserved. My sister thought it was letters. She was eleven and romantic and always looking for the version of adulthood that resembled the books she read. I didn't say what I thought. I had no theory. I just felt the drawer's presence the way you feel a word you can't remember — the shape of the absence more real than anything you could name.

We visited on Sundays. The whole extended machinery of the family arriving in shifts, cars parking crookedly on the narrow street, the sound of the screen door opening and closing all afternoon like a lung breathing. My grandmother presided over this without seeming to preside. She sat. She accepted things brought to her — plates of food, grandchildren placed temporarily in her lap, news delivered with varying degrees of drama. She gave the impression of a woman who had already seen the end of the story and was waiting, not impatiently, for everyone else to catch up.

She laughed easily but not at everything. There was a precision to it that I admired without understanding.

The kitchen. The drawer. Sunday.

Once, I came downstairs early, before anyone else was awake. Six in the morning, the house holding that particular silence of sleeping people, the air still carrying the memory of the night before. She was already up, standing at the stove, and for a moment she didn't hear me, or pretended not to. I watched her from the doorway. She was doing nothing unusual — stirring something, adjusting the heat — but she seemed different without an audience. Smaller, maybe, or more accurate. Like a word in its original language before translation.

She turned and saw me and her face did something. Not exactly surprise. More like a door closing, gently, without drama.

*Sit*, she said. *I'll make you something.*

I sat. She made eggs. We ate without talking much, which was unusual for her. She asked me something about school, I answered, she nodded. The drawer was behind me the entire time and I felt it the way you feel someone standing close.

I never opened it. I want to be clear about that. I had opportunities. Everyone has opportunities to do the thing they don't do. I'm not sure what stopped me. Respect, maybe, or something that looked like respect from the outside but was actually closer to fear. Fear of what I'd find, or fear of what I wouldn't. The possibility that the drawer contained nothing remarkable — some batteries, a few rubber bands, a takeout menu from a restaurant that had long since closed — felt almost worse than any secret.

Because what she had given us, without knowing it or maybe knowing it completely, was a space that belonged entirely to itself. Unobserved. Unverified. In a family that talked about everything, that processed and examined and revisited, here was one small rectangle of the world that remained its own.

She died on a Tuesday in November. We sorted through her things with the organized grief of a family that knows how to be useful. My aunt took the furniture. My mother took the photographs. Someone took the magnets off the refrigerator, though I don't know who.

Marcus called me the night it was all done. *We opened the drawer*, he said.

I waited.

*Just stuff*, he said. *A watch that didn't work. Some buttons. A birthday card we couldn't figure out who sent it.*

*That's all?*

*That's all.*

I said goodnight and I sat for a while in my apartment, which was quiet in the ordinary way apartments are quiet. I thought about the watch. A watch that didn't work, kept in a drawer, not thrown away. That seemed like information. That seemed like a whole life compressed into a small decision made once, long ago, and then simply maintained. *I will keep this. It doesn't work but I will keep it here.*

I think about her standing at the stove that morning, more accurate without an audience.

I think we are all most ourselves in the rooms where no one is watching, tending to the small things we've decided to keep for reasons we've stopped explaining even to ourselves.

The drawer was never really about what was in it.

She knew that. I think she wanted us to learn it without being told.

Some lessons have to be taught through absence. Through a step taken slightly faster past a particular place. Through thirty years of Sundays where everyone understood something that was never once said out loud.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Drawer at the Back of the Kitchen

There is a drawer in almost every kitchen I have ever been in that does not quite close all the way. It sticks at a particular angle, jutting out just enough that you catch your hip on it when you walk past in the dark, and you curse quietly and push it shut with the back of your hand, and three hours later it has drifted open again. Nobody fixes it. It becomes part of the house, like the step that creaks or the window that fogs in November and doesn't really clear until April. You stop seeing it after a while. You just absorb it into the way things are.

I have been thinking a lot lately about the things we absorb into the way things are.

There is something philosophically interesting happening in that drawer — or rather, in our relationship to that drawer. It represents a whole category of problems that are real, persistent, and entirely fixable, but which we have silently agreed to reclassify as features of the landscape rather than problems requiring solutions. We do this constantly. We are extraordinarily good at it. And I am genuinely uncertain whether this capacity is a form of wisdom or a form of defeat, and I suspect the honest answer is that it is both, operating simultaneously, and that the ratio shifts depending on the day you ask.

---

I want to talk about attention for a while, because I think about it constantly and I think we misunderstand what it is.

The standard way to discuss attention in our current cultural moment is to treat it as a resource under siege. Algorithms are stealing it. Phones are fragmenting it. The attention economy has turned our focus into a commodity being strip-mined by corporations with more computing power than most governments. All of this is true, and none of it is the most interesting thing about attention.

The most interesting thing about attention is how *creative* it is. We think of attention as a spotlight — you point it at a thing and you illuminate the thing. But attention is less like a spotlight and more like a hand. It doesn't just reveal what it touches. It shapes it. Changes it.
