The act of paying close attention to something is an act of participation in that thing's existence, at least as far as you are concerned.

I learned this from watching my grandfather look at birds.

He was not a formally educated man. He had left school at fifteen and spent most of his working life doing things with his hands — carpentry, mostly, but also stretches of farm work and a period I never fully understood that involved boats and the North Sea. But he had this habit, from as far back as anyone in the family could remember, of stopping completely when a bird landed nearby. Not just glancing. Stopping. He would become very still and watch the bird with total absorption, the way you sometimes see children watch things before they learn that sustained attention is somehow embarrassing.

What I noticed, watching him do this, was that the birds he described afterward were never the same birds I had seen. He would say something like, "Did you see the way it held its wing slightly off the body? It's been in a fight, maybe. Or it's just the heat." And I would realize I had seen a bird, categorized it as a sparrow or whatever, and moved on in about half a second. He had actually looked at the thing. He had seen an individual with a history and a body that bore evidence of its life.

He was not doing anything supernatural. He was just paying attention. But that attention was generative — it created a richer, more specific, more *true* version of the world than the one I was walking around in with my half-second glances and my taxonomic categories.

I think about this a lot when I read about the death of deep reading or the collapse of sustained concentration. The concern is usually framed as: we are losing the ability to focus for long enough to get through a novel or understand a complex argument. And that's a real concern. But the deeper loss might be something more like what my grandfather had with that bird — the capacity to let the specific reality of a particular thing actually land on you. To be changed, however slightly, by what you see.

---

There is a writer named Annie Dillard who said that how we spend our days is how we spend our lives. This is one of those sentences that reads as simple and then keeps expanding the more you sit with it.

The first time I encountered it, I thought: yes, obviously, time is cumulative, your daily habits become your biography, everyone knows this. But the sentence is doing something more unsettling than just reminding you to use your time well. It is collapsing the distinction between the meaningful and the mundane in a way that should disturb you.

Because what Dillard is really saying is that there is no other version of your life being held in reserve. There is no meaningful life that will begin once you get certain conditions in place. The Tuesday afternoon when you drove to the dry cleaner and picked up milk and sat in traffic and felt generally irritable and then watched something forgettable on your phone — that was your life. Not practice for your life. Not a pause in your life. Your actual life, happening in real time.

This can be read as depressing. I prefer to read it as freeing, though I won't pretend the freedom is comfortable.

What it means is that the question of how to live well cannot be deferred. It isn't waiting for a bigger canvas. It is being asked of you right now, in this moment, with these circumstances, in this kitchen with this drawer that won't close. The quality of your attention in ordinary moments is not separable from the quality of your life. They are the same thing.

---

I have been experimenting, off and on, with something I can only describe as deliberate noticing. This is not meditation, exactly, or not what I understand meditation to be — it doesn't involve any particular posture or breathing technique or conscious effort to clear the mind. It is more like occasionally, in the middle of ordinary activity, pausing to actually register what is happening.

The coffee. The specific weight of this mug. The way the light comes through this particular window at this particular hour and lands on this particular surface and does something with the steam rising from the coffee that is genuinely beautiful and that I have walked past probably a thousand times without seeing.

What I notice when I do this is not peace, exactly. It's more like an increase in resolution. The world gets more detailed. And then — this is the part that interests me most — I feel something that I can only call gratitude, but that doesn't require any object or recipient in the way the word usually implies. It's not gratitude *to* anyone for anything. It's more like the natural response of a mind that has stopped running away from the present long enough to actually find itself in it.

I don't do this consistently or well. Most days I am as distracted and forward-leaning as anyone, already in tomorrow before today is finished. But the moments when I manage it have a quality that makes me think this is something important — not in a grand sense, but in the way that finding the right key for a stuck lock is important. Something releases.

---

Let me tell you about something that bothers me.

We have built a culture that is extraordinarily sophisticated at producing distraction and extraordinarily unsophisticated at talking about what we actually want. I mean this seriously. We have podcasts about personal finance and productivity and psychological optimization and the habits of successful people, and what we have very little of is honest, non-self-help conversation about what makes a human life feel worth having.

I think we avoid this because the answers are embarrassing. Not embarrassing in a shameful way, but in a vulnerable way. The things that actually seem to constitute a felt sense of a life being well-lived are so ordinary that they are hard to say out loud without sounding like you're reading from a greeting card.

People. Being genuinely known by some people and genuinely knowing them in return. Not networking. Not maintaining relationships. Actually knowing someone — their specific anxieties and the particular way they laugh and what they're ashamed of and what they secretly think is hilarious.

Work that feels like it matters, where "matters" can be defined very humbly — it does not need to change the world, it just needs to have a why that you can feel in your chest on most mornings.

Some experience of the physical world that isn't mediated. Your body in weather. Your hands in soil or dough or water. The specific sensation of being a mammal on a planet, which we are all at risk of forgetting.

And time. The sense that time is yours. That you are living at your own pace rather than being fed through a process at someone else's.

None of this is new. Philosophers and grandmothers and monks and people sitting on porches have been saying versions of this for thousands of years. And yet we seem to require constant rediscovering of it, as if each generation has to learn it from scratch because it cannot be fully transmitted, only experienced.

---

I want to return to the drawer, because I think there's more in it.

The philosopher William James wrote about habit as the enormous flywheel of society. He meant it as a largely positive observation — habits are what let us function, what free up attention for things that require active thought. You don't have to consciously plan how to tie your shoes and therefore your mind is available for more interesting problems. Good.

But the same mechanism that automates your shoe-tying also automates your dissatisfactions. It turns the drawer from a problem into scenery. And then it turns other things into scenery. And then, if you're not careful, it turns your entire life into scenery — something that is happening in the background while you wait for the real thing to start.

I think the work of being a person — and I mean this in a very practical rather than mystical sense — is substantially about managing this automation. About deciding which things should be allowed to go automatic and which things you want to keep in focus. The drawer, fine. Let the drawer be scenery. But not the coffee. Not the bird. Not the specific face of the specific person sitting across from you, which you have stopped seeing because you have replaced it with your idea of them.

There's a Tolstoy story called "The Death of Ivan Ilyich" in which a man spends his life doing exactly the right things by every social measure — the right career, the right house decorated in the right way, the right social connections — and then gets sick and faces death and has to confront the possibility that he has not, actually, lived. The terror of the story is not the dying. It's the suspicion, confirmed too late, that he had let his life go automatic. That he had lived according to a script so completely that there was no Ivan Ilyich to be found inside the performance of Ivan Ilyich.

This story was written in 1886 and has never stopped being relevant for a single year since. Which tells you something.

---

What would it look like to un-automate a life? Or not fully un-automate — that's neither possible nor desirable — but to choose, more deliberately, what gets your actual attention?

I think it looks quiet and mostly unremarkable from the outside. It probably looks like someone who is sometimes slower than the situation requires. Who occasionally stops in the middle of something to notice the thing they're in the middle of. Who asks questions that seem slightly beside the point because the point they're interested in is different from the assumed point.

It looks like caring about the drawer. Not obsessing over it. Not writing long essays about it. Just — when you notice the drawer, actually noticing it. Making a small decision about whether to fix it or let it be. Owning that decision rather than just drifting past it again.

The accumulation of moments in which you are actually present and actually choosing — even in tiny, mundane ways — might be what a life feels like from the inside. As opposed to the sensation, which I think many people recognize, of arriving at some future moment and wondering how you got there without any sense of having traveled.

---

My grandfather died in the ordinary way that people die, in a hospital, at the end of a long illness, having had more than enough time to prepare and saying at various points that he felt he had done what he needed to do. I was young enough that the loss was sharp but my memory of it has softened into something that functions more like inheritance.

What I inherited from him, or what I think I'm still in the process of inheriting, is not a philosophy. It is more like a practice, or an orientation. The willingness to stop completely for a bird. To let the specific reality of the thing in front of you actually arrive.

He never would have described it in these terms. He didn't have the vocabulary for it and probably would have found the vocabulary annoying. He would have said he just liked birds. But I watched him, and what he was doing was something more than liking birds. He was paying the world the compliment of his full attention. And the world, in my observation, returned the favor by giving him more world than most people get.

---

The drawer in my kitchen opens to a depth of maybe eight inches and then stops. It is full of the things that drawers like this are always full of — take-out menus from places that no longer exist, batteries that might be dead, a flashlight I bought urgently once and now don't trust, some rubber bands, a cork, keys that fit nothing I can currently identify.

I have not fixed the drawer.

But I have decided to keep it. Not to fix it and not to remove it and not to file it in the category of Things That Are Simply The Way They Are. I have decided to let it be a small recurring reminder that attention is a choice and choices are cumulative and the life you are living is not a rehearsal, it is the only performance, and the audience is you.

Also, there is something almost companionable about a drawer that won't quite close. It has its own stubbornness. I respect that.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Invention of Elsewhere

I have been thinking lately about the peculiar human habit of going somewhere else in your mind while your body stays put.

You are sitting at a desk. The chair is a specific temperature against the backs of your thighs. The room has a smell — coffee, or dust, or the particular neutral smell of air conditioning that carries nothing at all. Your hands are resting on a surface. Everything about the physical situation is concrete and specific and irrefutably present. And then, without any machinery, without any chemical assistance, without moving a single voluntary muscle, you are somewhere else entirely. You are on a beach you visited when you were nine. You are in an argument that hasn't happened yet. You are in the middle of a novel's invented city, walking its invented streets with something that feels almost like the fatigue of actual walking.

This seems to me genuinely extraordinary, and I am not sure we treat it as such.

We have a word for the problematic version of this — we call it distraction, or dissociation, or daydreaming, the last of which carries a faint air of reproach, as though the dreaming part were fine but the day part represents a theft from productivity. We meditate in order to return to the present moment, which implies that the present moment is where we belong and these mental excursions are a kind of truancy. But I wonder if that framing gets it exactly backwards. I wonder if the capacity for mental elsewhere is not a malfunction of attention but the most fundamental thing about human consciousness — the thing that separates it most sharply from whatever a rock experiences, or a thermostat, or possibly even most animals.

The philosopher Derek Parfit spent decades thinking about personal identity — about what makes you the same person across time, about whether the self is a coherent thing at all or just a useful story we tell. He came to believe, roughly, that the self is much less unified and persistent than it feels, and he found this conclusion not distressing but liberating. If I am not really a solid continuous thing, then I am not really stuck. The boundaries between me and not-me are blurry. The walls are thinner than they appear.

I think the capacity for mental elsewhere is related to this — it is the mind's direct experience of its own permeability. When you are reading a novel and you feel, actually feel, the cold of a fictional winter, you are not being fooled. You are doing something real. You are demonstrating that experience is not tethered to the physical present the way we pretend it is. The elsewhere is real. It is just a different kind of real.

---

I want to talk about maps for a moment.

A map is not a place. This is the first and most important thing about maps, and also the thing that makes them possible. If a map were the place it represented, it would have to be exactly as large as the place, and it would be useless — you couldn't fold it, couldn't hold it at arm's length and see everything at once, couldn't plan a route by moving your finger from one point to another in a matter of seconds. The map works by being wrong in productive ways. It simplifies, distorts, leaves things out, emphasizes other things. The decisions about what to leave out are themselves a kind of argument about what matters.

Borges wrote a story, only a paragraph long, about an empire that created a map so detailed and accurate that it was the same size as the empire itself. The map was perfect. It was also useless, and it eventually rotted in the desert, and the animals made their nests in its ruins. The point Borges was making is the kind of point that seems obvious once someone makes it: perfection of representation is not the goal. The goal is a useful distortion.

Language is a map. Music is a map. Painting, mathematics, ritual, memory, friendship — all maps in this sense. All of them are wrong in ways that make them work. The word "dog" is not a dog. It does not have fur or a temperature. It does not pant. It is useful precisely because it is abstracted away from any particular dog, which means it can gesture at all of them, which means it can help you think about dogs in general, which would be impossible if every time you thought about a dog you had to fully imagine one specific dog complete with its idiosyncratic smell.

This is what the mind does when it goes elsewhere. It runs a map. It is not pretending the elsewhere is present — it is doing something stranger and more useful, which is holding the elsewhere at a scale where you can navigate it, turn it over, examine its edges. Memory is not playback. It is active reconstruction, which is why it changes every time you use it, why it is subject to suggestion and mood and what you had for breakfast. You are redrawing the map each time, which means each time the map is a little different, which is alarming from the perspective of accuracy and essential from the perspective of keeping the map alive and useful.

---

I find myself thinking about a particular afternoon when I was probably twelve or thirteen. I cannot date it exactly because memory is a map and maps don't always have good timestamps. I was lying on the floor of my bedroom — not on the bed, on the floor, for reasons I can no longer reconstruct, maybe the floor felt cool — and I was reading a book about astronomy. Not a textbook, one of those enthusiast books written by someone who clearly found the subject almost unbearably exciting and wanted you to feel that too. And there was a section about light travel time, about the fact that when you look at distant stars you are looking into the past, that the star might not even exist anymore, that the night sky is not a picture of now but a palimpsest of every different then.

I remember this as a genuine rupture in how I understood things. Not a big dramatic one — I didn't fall off the floor or cry or run to tell anyone. I just lay there on the carpet with the book open on my chest and felt the ceiling at a new distance. The ceiling was the same. The distance was new. Something had expanded.

I have had a handful of these moments and they share a quality I find hard to name. It is not quite surprise, because surprise is quick and this is slow. It is not quite pleasure, though it is not unpleasant. It is something more like the feeling of a room getting larger without its walls moving. A sense that the space available for thought has increased. I have come to believe these moments are the point — not just of reading or education, but of consciousness itself. The moments when the map gets redrawn to include more territory.

---

Here is something I think about: we are very bad at caring about large numbers.

Psychologists have documented this under the name "psychic numbing" — the phenomenon by which our emotional response to a tragedy does not scale with its size. We feel more for one identified person in distress than for a thousand statistical people in the same distress. We give more to a charity when we are told about a specific child than when we are told about a catastrophe affecting millions. The scope of the suffering actually seems to work against our engagement with it — the larger the number, the more abstract, the more distant, the less traction it gets on our actual decision-making.

This is usually presented as a flaw, and in many contexts it is. It means we are terrible at thinking about policy, terrible at responding to slow-moving large-scale disasters, terrible at making rational decisions about anything where the stakes are statistical rather than personal. But I have started to wonder if it also reveals something important about what we are actually built for.

We are not built for statistics. We are built for stories. More specifically, we are built for small groups — for tracking relationships, motivations, histories, for knowing who owes whom what and who can be trusted and who is likely to do what under which circumstances. The cognitive machinery that does this is ancient and specialized and very good. The cognitive machinery for thinking about a million of anything is a recent jury-rigged addition and it is not good at all.

What this means is that the natural habitat of the human mind is narrative. Not in the sense that we like stories (though we do), but in the sense that narrative structure — someone wants something, something is in the way, they try, they fail or succeed, there are consequences — is the fundamental schema through which we understand cause and effect, intention and outcome, the relationship between past and future. This is so deep that we apply it to things that have no intentions — we say the river wants to reach the sea, we say the plant is reaching for the light. We cannot help it. The story template is load-bearing.

And this means that all those mental elsewheres, all those maps we run in our heads of places and people and possible futures, are not distractions from thinking. They are thinking. The daydream where you rehearse a difficult conversation is not an indulgence — it is practice, it is simulation, it is your brain doing exactly what it evolved to do. The novel that makes you cry about people who do not exist is not fooling you — it is giving you navigable maps of emotional territories you haven't visited yet, or territories you have visited and never found words for.

---

There is a kind of place I have always loved, which is the edge of things. Coastlines. The last house at the end of a road. The hour just before dawn. These are liminal things, threshold things — they have the quality of existing between categories, and I find that quality compelling in a way I have only gradually come to understand.

I think what I love is that at the edge, both sides are still visible. On a coastline you can see the land behind you and the ocean ahead. At dawn you can see the dark that is leaving and the light that is arriving. At the last house on the road, you can see the built world you came from and the unbuilt world you are about to enter. The edge is the only place where you have this double vision, this awareness of what is and what might be simultaneously.

This might be why I am drawn to first drafts and rough sketches and the version of a sentence before it knows what it wants to say. The finished thing is sure of itself, which is part of its value, but it has lost the particular aliveness of not-yet-sure. The sketch has all its options still available. The first draft might still become anything.

Somewhere in here is why I find travel meaningful and also why I find it overrated. Travel gives you new edges — you are suddenly at the border between your home map and a foreign map, and the friction between them is productive. Things you assumed were universal turn out to be local. Things you assumed were local turn out to be universal. The ceiling gets a new distance. But travel can also become a kind of acquisitiveness — adding countries to a list, adding experiences to a portfolio, staying just long enough to take the photograph but not long enough for the map to actually redraw. The elsewhere has to be dwelt in, not just witnessed.

---

I said earlier that I have been thinking about the human habit of going somewhere else in your mind, and that I find it extraordinary. I want to be more specific about the nature of the extraordinary.

What I mean is this: the universe is very old and very large, and for almost all of its age and in almost all of its extent, nothing in it has had any experience of being anywhere or doing anything. The rocks do not wonder what it is like to be a rock. The stars do not feel their own light leaving. For billions of years there was no perspective anywhere — just matter and energy doing what matter and energy do, following rules, producing outcomes, building complexity.

And then, on at least one planet, from the complexity emerged something that could take a point of view. Something that could stand somewhere specific and look out. And then, after more time, something that could imagine standing somewhere else. Could hold in its mind a there that was not here, a then that was not now, a might-be that was not yet. Could write it down, which meant another point of view could receive it, and for a moment two minds could be somewhere that neither of them physically was.

This strikes me as one of the most important things that has ever happened, and I do not think we have a fully adequate response to it. We take it for granted because it is so close to us, because we have never known what it is like to not have an inner life. We cannot feel the contrast. We are like fish who have never heard of water.

But occasionally, in certain moods, the contrast becomes available. Sometimes when I am reading something that is doing what only language can do — reaching into me and rearranging things, making a room larger without moving its walls — I catch a glimpse of the improbability. That this is possible at all. That minds can meet this way. That elsewhere can be made real through nothing more than arranged marks on a surface, or arranged vibrations in air.

Consciousness invented elsewhere. It reached beyond the present moment and found that it could. And then it reached beyond its own experience and found other minds to reach with. And none of this was inevitable — it was the result of a billion small increments of increasing complexity, each of which could have gone differently, on one planet around one star in one galaxy among two trillion.

And here we are. Going somewhere else in our minds. Redrawing maps. Finding new distances in familiar ceilings.

I don't think we have an adequate word for the appropriate response to this fact. Gratitude is too small. Awe comes closer. What I really mean is something like: the full weight of attention. The willingness to look at what is actually happening and refuse to find it ordinary.

The elsewhere is real. The maps are how we live. The edge is where both sides are visible.

That seems like enough.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Smell of Old Books and What It Means to Lose a Sense

I want to talk about petrichor first, because petrichor is one of those words that people discover and immediately want to use in a sentence, any sentence, just to have said it. *The smell of rain on dry earth.* There's a compound responsible for it — geosmin, produced by soil bacteria called actinomycetes — and knowing this chemical fact somehow makes the smell both more and less interesting at the same time. More interesting because the earth is literally exhaling microbial breath at you when rain falls. Less interesting because naming a thing is the first step toward making it ordinary.

But petrichor is what I'm thinking about as I try to work out what I actually want to write about, which is olfaction — the sense of smell — and specifically the strange cultural position it occupies. Smell is the most emotionally loaded of the senses, tied more directly to memory than any other, routing through the limbic system in ways that vision and hearing simply don't. And yet it's also the sense we talk about the least, the one we have the fewest words for, the one that embarrasses us sometimes, the one that COVID-19 stole from millions of people and in doing so made suddenly, desperately precious.

Let me start with the books.

---

There's a word — *bibliosmia* — which refers to the smell of old books, though the word itself is barely in any dictionary and probably functions more as a pleasing neologism than established vocabulary. But the thing it describes is real and widely beloved. You walk into a second-hand bookshop or an old library and there's something waiting for you — vanilla and almonds and something faintly woody, something that smells like time itself if time had a texture. Chemists have actually studied this. The smell is produced by the slow degradation of paper: lignin breaking down into vanillin, which is chemically related to vanilla. Older paper releases benzaldehyde, which smells of almonds. There are hundreds of volatile organic compounds involved. The smell of old books is the smell of decay, technically. It is the slow chemical process of dissolution making itself known to you in the most romantic possible way.

This seems significant to me. The thing we find comforting and nostalgic and associated with knowledge and warmth is fundamentally a process of disintegration. The book is telling you it's dying. And we breathe it in and feel peaceful.

There's something in that dynamic — smell revealing truths that other senses dress up or conceal — that keeps drawing me back. Vision, after all, is extraordinarily easy to manipulate. We understand this. Cinema is built on the manipulation of vision. Advertising rewires how things look. We spend extraordinary effort making things appear different from what they are. Hearing is somewhat harder to deceive but still remarkably tractable — we pipe music into spaces specifically to alter how people feel, how long they stay, how much they spend. Smell is trickier. We can add smells — casinos reportedly pump oxygen and certain fragrances to keep people alert and betting; supermarkets pipe bread smells from the bakery throughout the store — but there's a rawness to smell, an inability to completely fake it, that means it keeps delivering true information. A building that smells of mold is moldy, no matter how fresh the paint looks. A person who smells of fear — and this is a real thing, not just a metaphor; people do emit different compounds when frightened — is frightened, even if their face is composed. The body broadcasts on frequencies that vision can't jam.

---

Helen Keller wrote about smell as "the fallen angel of the senses." She had reason to think carefully about senses that most people take for granted, and her conclusion was that smell was the most underappreciated, the one that could have given the most and was instead mostly ignored. She described smelling a passing stranger and being able to construct a whole person from that information — their profession, their recent activities, something of their character. Whether this was literallyaccurate or somewhat lyrical, I don't know. But it points toward something real: smell is informative in ways we've trained ourselves to stop registering.

Animals haven't made this error. A dog's olfactory epithelium has about 300 million receptor cells compared to a human's 6 million. A dog can detect certain cancers, track a person across terrain days later, identify a family member in a crowd from a scent alone. We tend to position this as a remarkable dog fact rather than a human deficiency, but maybe that framing is wrong. Maybe the more useful observation is that humans, somewhere in the course of becoming the cognitive creatures we became, partially abandoned one of their richest information channels. We built language instead, which is remarkable and transformative and allows me to write this sentence, but also substitutes a described world for a directly experienced one in ways that are not always improvements.

The anthropologist Constance Classen has argued that the history of smell in Western culture is largely a history of increasing suppression. Older cultures — and many contemporary non-Western cultures — organized social life much more explicitly around smell. Certain smells were sacred, certain smells were profane. Aromatics were political. Frankincense and myrrh were valuable partly because burning them created a specific olfactory environment that signaled the presence of the sacred, that altered the mood and physiology of everyone in the space. The Western Enlightenment, with its emphasis on vision as the rational sense — on observation, on the visible, on the eye of reason — pushed smell toward the background. Smell became associated with the irrational, the animal, the bodily, the embarrassing. We perfumed ourselves to mask natural smells and then felt vaguely ashamed of the perfume too.

Meanwhile, the vocabulary failed to develop. English has almost no primary smell words — words that specifically denote a smell without reference to its source or to another sense. *Pungent* means penetrating, which is a tactile metaphor. *Acrid* refers to sharpness, another tactile metaphor. *Fragrant* just means pleasant-smelling without specifying what kind of pleasant. Compare this to the color vocabulary, or the vocabulary for sounds, or taste. We have *sweet, sour, bitter, salty, umami* as primary taste categories with wide cultural elaboration. We have hue and saturation and brightness and an endless proliferation of named colors. We have pitch and timbre and rhythm for sound. For smell, we mostly say "it smells like..." and then name the source: it smells like roses, it smells like rain, it smells like something burning. We are pointing rather than describing. We are using our smell vocabulary to defer to vision — the named object — rather than to characterize the sensation directly.

Some languages do better. The Jahai people of the Malay Peninsula have been studied by linguist Asifa Majid, who found they possess a rich vocabulary for smells that allows them to describe olfactory qualities as abstractly and precisely as English speakers describe colors. They are not pointing at sources; they are naming properties. This suggests the poverty is cultural and linguistic, not inherent. We could have built the vocabulary. We chose differently.

---

Which brings me to what happened when COVID-19 removed the sense of smell from millions of people.

Anosmia — the loss of smell — had always existed, caused by injury, illness, aging, various other factors. There is a small and genuinely devoted community of people who live with it permanently, who have been trying to explain to the olfactory-equipped majority what it's like. And what they describe is not simply the absence of smell. It is the absence of an entire emotional register. Food becomes fuel. Memory loses a trigger. Arriving home loses something. The world becomes slightly flattened in a way that's difficult to convey to people who haven't experienced it, because you can't demonstrate the absence of a sense to someone who still has it.

When COVID took smell from people who had previously not thought much about it, the accounts were striking. Person after person describing not just the missing smells but the missing *attachment to the world*. The smell of a newborn child. The smell of a partner's skin. The smell of coffee, not as a treat but as a daily recalibration, a sensory event that says: this is the morning, this is the beginning, you are here. When these were removed, people reported not just inconvenience but something closer to grief. A flattening of affect. A sense of being behind glass.

The philosopher of mind might note here that this suggests smell is doing more emotional and cognitive work than we typically give it credit for. We think of attention as a resource we deploy consciously, but many of our attention-orienting mechanisms are sensory and automatic. Smell is constantly reading the environment and generating low-level emotional responses — comfort, alertness, unease, appetite — that we experience as moods or dispositions without knowing their source. Remove smell and the mood regulation suffers. You don't know why you feel slightly less grounded. The signal is gone but you don't have language for the signal so you can't name the absence.

There's also the memory dimension, which is probably the most discussed aspect of olfaction even though it remains somewhat mysterious mechanically. The connection between smell and memory is not subtle. Proust built the most famous passage in French literature around it — the madeleine dipped in tea, releasing a smell that unlocks an entire childhood — and while it's become a slightly tired reference, the phenomenon itself is genuine and replicable and strange. Smell memories are often older and more emotionally charged than memories encoded through other senses. They bypass the same filtering that other sensory memories pass through. They arrive with an urgency, a presentness, that can be disorienting: you are suddenly *there*, in that kitchen, in that school hallway, at that moment, with a vividness that visual memory usually doesn't provide.

Why? The anatomy offers a partial answer. The olfactory nerve connects directly to the amygdala and hippocampus — the brain regions most centrally involved in emotion and memory — without the relay through the thalamus that other senses require. Smell has a more direct line to the emotional core of the brain. It was there before the cerebral cortex elaborated into its current complexity. It is an old system talking to other old systems, without the neocortical intermediary that processes and moderates and rationalizes. When a smell triggers a memory, you're not retrieving and reconstructing in the way that recalling a visual scene involves; you're being pulled somewhere, as if the smell were still happening, as if you were still there.

This is why anosmia, in people who develop it after a lifetime of smelling, produces what some researchers have called a kind of autobiographical disruption. The smell-memory connection isn't just about pleasant nostalgic moments. It's about the continuous sense of having been a continuous person — of the present being connected to the past through something immediate and sensory. Take that away and the timeline gets thinner.

---

I keep returning to something that feels like a larger point about the things we don't have words for.

The weakness of smell vocabulary is an example of a more general truth: that we navigate by what we can name, and anything that falls outside the naming system becomes difficult to think about and easy to ignore. This isn't a novel observation — Wittgenstein gestured at it, Orwell worried about it in a political register, contemporary cognitive scientists study it empirically — but it seems to acquire a particular vividness when you're thinking about smell, because smell is such a clear example of a channel of genuine richness that we've let go partly fallow.

What else have we let go fallow? What other information streams are we receiving but not processing, not discussing, not developing vocabularies for? Interoception — the sense of the internal state of the body, the vague sense of one's own physiology — is probably another. We're not great at it. We're famously poor at knowing when we're hungry versus when we're thirsty, when we're tired versus when we're sad, when our blood sugar is low versus when we're anxious. The body is talking continuously and we attend intermittently, when the signal gets loud enough to break through.

There's probably a version of this essay to be written about every sense that gets undervalued, every information channel we have that we've failed to fully develop. But smell feels like the most poignant case because it sits at this odd intersection: most directly tied to memory and emotion, most lacking in vocabulary, most connected to things we care about enormously — connection to places, connection to people, the texture of daily life — and somehow least discussed, least celebrated, most taken for granted right up until it's gone.

When it's gone, people understand what it was. This is the common human structure: understanding a thing through its absence. Grief teaches you what love was doing. Illness teaches you what health was doing. Anosmia, it turns out, teaches you that smell was doing the work of continuous orientation — that it was quietly affirming, thousands of times a day, that you were somewhere specific, that specific things were happening, that you were present in the world.

The old books smell of their own dying. The rain smells of living soil. The morning coffee says *here, now, you*. And none of it requires a word from you. It is just there, operating, doing its patient work, connecting you to everything that has ever smelled like this to you, sending its chemical messages along the oldest pathways in your brain, asking nothing except that you breathe.

Which is, it turns out, not nothing.

---

I don't have a tidy conclusion. The sense of smell doesn't resolve into a lesson. What I'm left with is something more like an instruction: pay attention to it, the way you'd pay attention to something you knew you might lose. Smell the old books. Smell the rain. Smell the people you love, which sounds strange until you've lost it and then sounds like the most basic thing in the world.

Develop whatever vocabulary you can. *This smells like Saturday morning. This smells like every good summer. This smells like safety.* Even if the words are clumsy and referring to sources rather than qualities, even if you're pointing rather than describing, it's better than silence. The naming is a way of registering. The registering is a way of keeping.

The fallen angel of the senses. The one that takes the most direct path to where you actually live.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I've been thinking about maps lately. Not the kind you pull up on your phone when you can't find a parking garage, but the older kind — the ones that were wrong in interesting ways. Medieval cartographers would draw sea monsters in the unmapped ocean. *Here be dragons*, the famous warning, though historians argue whether that phrase actually appeared on old maps or whether we've collectively misremembered it into existence, which is itself a fascinating problem, and one I want to spend some time with.

Because the maps we carry in our heads are the ones I'm actually interested in. The internal ones. The geography of memory and how it fails us in ways that are sometimes catastrophic and sometimes beautiful.

---

There's a phenomenon called *confabulation* that neurologists first identified in patients with certain kinds of brain damage. When a person confabulates, they produce false memories without any intent to deceive. They're not lying. They genuinely believe what they're saying. A patient with Korsakoff's syndrome, brought on typically by chronic alcoholism and thiamine deficiency, might tell you a detailed story about where they were last Tuesday — a fishing trip, specific weather, the smell of the lake — when in fact they were sitting in a hospital ward the entire time. The story isn't random noise. It's coherent, structured, emotionally plausible. It sounds like memory because it uses all the same machinery as memory. The brain, finding a gap, fills it. Not with static, but with narrative.

What unnerves me about confabulation isn't the pathological version. It's the implication for the rest of us.

Because we do this too. Constantly. The research on memory over the past several decades has been quietly devastating to the intuitive picture most people carry around of how remembering works. We tend to think of memories as files — stored, retrievable, more or less intact. You put something in, you take it back out. Maybe the image degrades a little, the way a photocopy of a photocopy loses resolution. But the file is the file. The event happened, you encoded it, you stored it, you can access it.

This is almost entirely wrong.

Memory is reconstructive, not reproductive. Every time you remember something, you're not playing back a recording. You're rebuilding the event from fragments, and the act of rebuilding changes the fragments. You are, each time you remember, slightly rewriting what happened. Memories are more like sculptures you keep returning to with a chisel than photographs in an album. And the chisel takes off a little material each visit, smooths some edges, deepens others, sometimes accidentally carves something new altogether.

Elizabeth Loftus spent decades demonstrating this in labs. She could implant entirely false memories in research subjects — memories of being lost in a shopping mall as a child, of seeing a wounded animal, of experiences that never happened — and the subjects would hold these memories with confidence, often embellishing them, defending them. The memory felt like memory. It had the phenomenal quality of remembering. But the event it pointed to had never occurred.

I don't bring this up to induce existential dread, though it might. I bring it up because I think there's something important hiding in the implication.

---

If memory is reconstruction, then the act of remembering is, in some small but real sense, an act of imagination. Not fabrication — I want to be careful here. Most of our memories track reality closely enough to be useful. We remember people we've actually known, places we've actually been, things that actually happened. The distortions are usually at the margins, in the details, in the emotional coloring. But the *process* of remembering shares fundamental machinery with the process of imagining. The brain regions involved overlap substantially. When you picture your grandmother's kitchen and when you picture a kitchen you've never visited, you are using much of the same neural hardware. The difference isn't architectural; it's in the signal, something about whether this particular reconstruction is tagged as *real* or *invented*.

And that tag is not infallible.

So here's where I find myself: if imagination and memory share a substrate, and if memory is constantly being revised by subsequent experience, emotion, suggestion, and the distorting pressure of narrative — the need to have been a coherent person with a coherent story — then the line between who I am and who I have imagined myself to be is genuinely blurry. Not blurry in a fun, philosophical, dinner-party way. Blurry in a structural, inescapable way.

The story I tell about my past shapes the past itself. This sounds mystical but it isn't. It's mechanics. When I narrate my history, I consolidate certain memories (making them stronger and more distorted), let others atrophy, draw connections that weren't present in the original experience, assign causes and motivations that weren't available to me at the time. I do this automatically, constantly. Everyone does. We are all, to a significant degree, the authors of our own pasts, and we don't fully realize it because the writing feels like reading.

---

This connects to something I find genuinely strange about the concept of personal growth.

We talk about people changing — becoming better, wiser, more compassionate — and we celebrate this. A person who was cruel in youth but kind in age, we find that redemptive. But we also talk about people remaining *themselves* through change, there being some continuous thread of identity that persists despite transformation. And these two ideas sit in genuine tension that we mostly paper over.

If the self is substantially constructed from memory, and memory is malleable, then the self is malleable in a way that should be more troubling to our sense of personal continuity than we typically allow. The person I was at fifteen is not merely younger and less experienced than the person I am now. He is, in some meaningful sense, a character I have been writing and rewriting for decades. I have access to him only through reconstructive memory, which means I have access to him only through the distorting lens of everything I became afterward. I cannot see him fresh. He is always already interpreted.

And yet I feel continuous with him. I feel, when I think about being fifteen, something like *that was me*. Not just genetically, not just physically, but in some inner sense that resists easy analysis. There's a phenomenological thread. There's something it was like to be him that I can partially access, imperfectly, through the act of remembering, which is to say through the act of reconstruction.

I wonder sometimes if this feeling of continuity is itself a kind of confabulation. Not false in its entirety, but built partly from narrative necessity. We need to be the same person over time in order to function socially, legally, morally. You can be held responsible for what you did because you are — must be — the same person who did it. Societies are built on this. Relationships are built on it. Without it, the entire structure of accountability collapses. So we construct the continuity. We hold it together through story, through the ongoing act of autobiography, the constant low-level narration that most of us run throughout our waking lives. *I am the kind of person who. I have always been someone who. I never could have.*

The maps we make of ourselves, in other words, are as distorted as the old maps with the sea monsters. We fill in the gaps with plausible monsters of our own.

---

Here's what I keep returning to, though: the distortion isn't only loss. Something important happens in the rewriting.

There's a version of the argument about malleable memory that ends in nihilism or at least radical skepticism. If memory isn't reliable, if the self is more constructed than continuous, if we're all confabulating our way through our own histories — then what's solid? What can be trusted? It can read as a kind of unraveling.

But I think that's the wrong conclusion, or at least an incomplete one.

Consider what it means that memory is reconstructive rather than reproductive. It means that memory is *alive*. It changes in response to who you are now. The past is not sealed. The significance of what happened to you is not fixed at the moment of occurrence. A humiliation you suffered at twelve might be, at forty, something you've metabolized into a source of empathy. A triumph at twenty might be, at fifty, something that embarrasses you with its smallness. The meaning of events keeps updating. The past keeps changing its shape in the light of the present.

This is why people can be genuinely healed, genuinely transformed, through therapy, through meditation, through relationships, through art — through any process that changes the story they tell about what happened. It's not delusion. It's how the machinery works. The trauma you carry isn't a perfect recording. It's a reconstruction that has been built and rebuilt in particular ways, often by the same survival instincts and protective distortions that got you through the original experience. Rebuilding it differently — gently, carefully, with help — is working with the actual material, not bypassing it.

The map can be redrawn. Here there be dragons — but you've been to that part of the ocean now, and it turns out the dragons are something different than you thought, and they're not in that location anyway, they're somewhere else, and also you have more tools than you knew.

---

The medieval cartographers who drew sea monsters in the unknown regions weren't simply being credulous. Some of them, surely, were reflecting genuine uncertainty — this is the place where things we don't understand might happen. The monster is a marker for the edge of knowledge, a symbol that means *past here, I can't help you*. There's a strange honesty in that. Modern maps don't have that. Modern maps have the confident blank white of unpopulated tundra or the pixelated blue of ocean, and they say nothing about the limits of what is known. They simply represent, within those limits, what has been surveyed. The unknown doesn't get represented at all. It just doesn't appear.

I'm not sure that's an improvement.

We've lost something in gaining accuracy. Or rather — accuracy has costs, and one of them is the disappearance of the visible edge. We no longer know, from the map, where we're leaving known territory. The map just ends, and there's no monster to warn you.

The internal maps — the autobiographical ones — still have their monsters. The regions of memory that are most distorted tend to be the ones most saturated with emotion, particularly fear and shame. The cartography of the self tends to be least accurate precisely in the areas that matter most. The highly charged events of early life, the formative experiences that shape how we move through the world — these are the regions where the sea monsters live, where the confabulation is thickest, where the reconstruction has been most heavily influenced by subsequent need and emotion.

Which means the most important thing to know about yourself is probably also the hardest to see clearly. Not impossible. Not hopeless. But requiring a particular kind of effort — the willingness to go back into territory you've mapped, with the understanding that the map is wrong, and to look again without assuming you already know what's there.

---

I don't want to end on something pat. I've been thinking through this because it genuinely unsettles me, not because I've arrived at a comfortable resolution.

There's something vertiginous about taking seriously the idea that you don't have direct access to your own past. That the person you think you were, the experiences you think you had, the reasons you think you have for being the way you are — all of this is mediated, reconstructed, revised, partly invented. It makes solid ground seem less solid. It makes the confident self-knowledge we mostly proceed on look like a necessary fiction, which is not quite the same as a lie but is also not the same as knowledge.

And yet — here is the strangest part — the unsettlement itself feels useful. There's something orienting about knowing the map is imperfect. It changes how you hold your own story. You grip it a little less tightly. You become a little more curious about whether you've got it right, a little less defensive when it's challenged. You develop what might be called *epistemic humility about the self*, though that phrase sounds more serene and philosophical than the actual experience of it, which can feel like walking on a floor you're not sure about.

What I keep coming back to is the image of the cartographer at the edge of the known world, looking out at the water, and making a decision about what to draw. They could leave it blank. They could draw what they imagined. They could draw a monster to mean *I don't know*. All of those are choices with consequences. All of those shapes on the map will influence the behavior of the next sailor who uses it.

We are all making those choices, constantly, in the maps we draw of ourselves. What to fill in. What to leave blank. Where to put the dragons.

I think the best I can do — the best anyone can do — is to stay suspicious of the smooth, highly detailed sections. The parts of your own story that feel most certain, most coherent, most polished are exactly the parts that have been handled most, revised most, narrated most often. They are the most worked. And things that have been worked that much carry the worker's fingerprints.

The real territory might look quite different from the map. It usually does.

That's frightening, and it might also be the beginning of something. A different kind of looking. A willingness to go back out to the edge and admit that the monsters we drew there were our own invention, and that the actual ocean, the one that was always out there, is still largely unknown.

---

Here be, potentially, something else entirely.

That seems worth knowing.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I want to write about maps.

Not maps in the practical sense — not the folded paper rectangles that used to avalanche out of glove compartments, not the digital blue dot sliding across a phone screen like a cursor finding its place in the world. I want to write about the other kind of maps. The ones we make inside ourselves. The ones that tell us where we've been and, more importantly, whether those places still exist.

Here is something that has occupied my thinking more than I can reasonably justify: the fact that every map is already out of date the moment it is finished. Cartographers have always known this. The world refuses to hold still long enough to be accurately recorded. Rivers migrate. Coastlines erode. Cities expand in ways that make their old boundaries look quaint, like the outline of a child's hand traced in crayon and then compared to the adult hand that grew from it. There is a word — *palimpsest* — for a manuscript that has been written on, scraped clean, and written on again, where the old text bleeds through the new one in ghostly traces. Every map is a palimpsest. Every place is a palimpsest. And I think every person is too.

I grew up in a house that no longer exists in the form I knew it. This is not a tragedy — it is simply the standard operating procedure of time. The family that lives there now has painted the walls different colors. The enormous pine tree in the backyard, the one I used to climb until I was high enough to look down on the roof with a complicated feeling of both terror and sovereignty, was cut down years ago. A neighbor mentioned it in passing, casual as remarking on weather, and the news arrived with a disproportionate weight. A tree I had not thought about in years suddenly became the most important tree that had ever existed. Its absence created a presence.

This is what I mean about the cartography of forgetting.

We carry internal maps of every place we have ever mattered to. And those maps have a strange property: they preserve not just the physical layout — the placement of furniture, the particular creak of a specific stair, the way light fell through a west-facing window in late October — but also the emotional topography. The places in the house that felt safe. The corner where anxiety lived. The doorway you always stood in when a difficult conversation was happening because standing in a doorway is a way of being half-in and half-out of a thing, and sometimes that ambiguity is the only honest position available.

The philosopher Gaston Bachelard wrote a book called *The Poetics of Space* in which he argued that houses are not just shelters but are actually the first universe of the human self. The house shapes the imagination. The child who grows up with an attic grows up with a different inner life than the child who does not — the attic-child has a space for the vertigo of height, for dust, for stored and forgotten things, for the sense that above the ordinary world there is another level, dimmer and more mysterious. I find this persuasive. I find it almost uncomfortably persuasive, because it means that the spaces we inhabit early do something to us that cannot be easily undone, that the architecture of our childhood becomes in some sense the architecture of our minds.

And then those spaces change, or disappear, or are inhabited by strangers who rearrange everything according to their own interior needs, and we are left holding a map that no longer corresponds to any territory.

---

I want to talk about something related, which is the experience of returning.

There is a specific and somewhat cruel quality to revisiting a place you loved when you were younger. You arrive with your old map in hand, confident in its accuracy, and the territory immediately begins its quiet assault on your certainties. The distances are wrong — everything is closer together than memory insisted. The grand hallway of your elementary school is just a hallway. The hill you used to sled down, the one that felt in memory like something out of the Alps, is a perfectly modest incline that any adult would walk up without breaking stride. Scale is the first casualty of return.

But then something stranger happens. If you stay long enough, if you stand in the place and let your attention go soft rather than sharp, the old scale reasserts itself. You can feel it: the remembered largeness of things pressing against the actual smallness of them. You exist in two times simultaneously. You are both the child for whom this hill was a mountain and the adult who can see plainly that it is not. And the strange mercy of this double vision is that it makes you briefly real to yourself in a way that ordinary daily life often fails to do. You are the sum of all your ages at once. You are every version of yourself that ever stood here, superimposed.

This is why people make pilgrimages. Not the religious ones necessarily — though those too — but the ordinary secular pilgrimages that we don't always name as such. The drive past the house you grew up in. The walk through the neighborhood where you fell in love for the first time, or where you were unhappy in a formative way. We go back to these places not because we expect them to be unchanged but because we need to check the map against the territory. We need the friction of that comparison. It does something necessary.

What does it do? I've been trying to articulate this for years and I still don't have a clean answer. The closest I can come is this: returning to a place collapses the distance between who you were and who you are, and that collapse is briefly painful and briefly clarifying and ultimately, maybe, a form of integration. You are putting the past into the present tense. You are telling yourself: this happened. I was there. I was that person and now I am this person and both are real.

---

Here is where I want to go next, and I recognize it is a bit of a leap, but I trust the associative logic.

I think about this cartographic problem — the map that persists after the territory has changed — in relation to people as much as places. The people we have known intimately get mapped. We develop detailed, confident internal representations of them: their patterns, their fears, their specific ways of moving through a room or deflecting a question or expressing joy. We know them. And then time passes and they change, as people do, and we keep consulting our old map rather than surveying the new territory.

This might be the source of so many of the disappointments and failures of long relationships. Not malice. Not indifference. Just cartographic lag. You are navigating by an outdated map and wondering why you keep running aground.

The novelist Marilynne Robinson has a line somewhere — I'm going to misquote it because I am working from memory, which is itself a kind of cartography — about how the living and the dead occupy the same space in the mind, how the people we love who are gone remain present in a way that is not metaphorical but structurally real, actual presences in actual consciousness. I think about this a lot. Because the same is true, in a lesser key, of people who are still alive but who have moved out of your daily life. The college friend you are still close to in theory but see once every few years. The parent who has aged past the version of them you most viscerally remember. The person you were in a relationship with who is now rearranging their own furniture, making their own coffee, becoming a stranger who knows everything about you.

These people persist in the mind with a vividness and specificity that is sometimes startling. You catch a piece of music they loved and they are suddenly completely there, three-dimensional, more present than many of the people you will speak to today. The map hasn't faded. The territory has simply moved on without it.

---

I want to say something about beauty before I finish, because I think it connects.

One of the things I find most interesting about beautiful things — a piece of music, a painting, a landscape, a line of writing that stops you cold — is that they seem to operate by a similar mechanism to the one I've been describing. They create a kind of cartographic event. They give you a sudden precise map of something that you hadn't known needed mapping. You hear a particular chord progression and something in you says: *yes, that is exactly what that feels like*. You didn't know you needed a map for that feeling. You didn't even know the feeling had a geography. And then there it is, named in musical terms, charted.

This is why art feels like recognition rather than discovery. Why people cry at music that isn't sad, why certain paintings make people feel understood rather than merely aesthetically pleased. The map that the artwork offers corresponds to some interior territory you've been wandering in without knowing where you were. The artwork gives you your location. Suddenly you know where you are.

And then — here's the part that I find both beautiful and melancholy — you walk away from the artwork and back into ordinary life, and the map fades a little. Not completely, never completely, but enough that the precision dims. You try to find your way back to that clarity, back to that exact recognition, and you can get close, you can find the neighborhood, but the exact address is harder. This is why people listen to songs they love hundreds of times. They are returning. They are checking the map.

---

I have been thinking about something a philosopher named Derek Parfit wrote about personal identity, which is the question of what makes you the same person over time. He thought the common intuitions about this were basically confused. There isn't some essential self that persists through time like a bead on a string. What there is instead is a series of connected selves, related to each other by continuity of memory and character and intention, but not identical. The you of twenty years ago is related to the you of now the way — he used this comparison — the England of the fourteenth century is related to the England of today. Connected, continuous, but not the same entity.

If Parfit is right, then all of us are always carrying outdated maps of ourselves. The self we believe we are navigating by is already a few versions out of date. The fears we think we have might be inherited from a version of ourselves that no longer exists. The self-knowledge we're most confident in might be the oldest and therefore the most suspect.

This is either terrifying or liberating, and I have not entirely decided which. Some days it seems like good news: the things about yourself you most wish were different are not permanent features of some fixed essential you, they are features of a temporary configuration that is already changing. Some days it seems like bad news: the things about yourself you most treasure are equally impermanent, equally subject to erosion and revision.

Maybe the useful response to both possibilities is the same: hold the map loosely. Be willing to go back and survey the territory again. Be suspicious of your own confident knowledge — of places, of other people, of yourself — not because knowledge is impossible but because certainty is its enemy. The most accurate maps are the ones that include their own date of creation. The most honest self-knowledge includes the acknowledgment: *this is what I knew about myself when I made this map, and the world has continued moving.*

---

Outside my window — wherever that window is, whenever this is being read, in whatever configuration of light and season — the world is doing what it always does, which is continuing. Streets are being walked on. Trees are growing incrementally in ways that won't be noticeable for years, and then one day someone will stand under a tree and think: when did that get so big? A child is memorizing the geography of a house that will become, decades from now, the interior architecture of their imagination. Someone is returning to a place they left and feeling the double exposure of then and now. Someone is hearing a piece of music that gives them a sudden precise map of something they didn't know could be mapped.

All of us are navigating by documents of varying accuracy. All of us are partly lost and partly found, holding our inherited maps up against a landscape that keeps suggesting, gently or urgently, that we might want to check our coordinates.

I find this not entirely comforting and not entirely frightening. I find it mostly true. And I have a weakness for true things even when — especially when — they resist resolution, when they sit with their own complexity without apologizing for it, when they point at something real and say: here, this is here, this has always been here, you have been walking through it without quite seeing it.

The map is not the territory. The territory is not the map. And the distance between them — that gap, that creative and melancholy and sometimes beautiful gap — is where almost everything interesting happens.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
# The Strange Comfort of Unfinished Things

There is a drawer in my mind — I suspect you have one too — where unfinished things accumulate. Half-read books with cracked spines. Languages studied for three weeks in January and then abandoned somewhere around the subjunctive tense. Recipes torn from magazines and never cooked. Apologies rehearsed in the shower and never delivered. The drawer doesn't close properly anymore. It bulges.

We tend to treat this drawer as evidence of failure, a catalog of our inadequacies, proof that we are the kind of people who start things and don't finish them. Self-help culture has made a minor industry out of this anxiety. Close your open loops. Finish what you start. Eliminate the incomplete. The underlying assumption is that completion is the goal, the destination, the only legitimate outcome of any endeavor.

But I've been thinking lately that this assumption deserves more scrutiny than we usually give it.

Consider the Japanese concept of *wabi-sabi*, the aesthetic philosophy that finds beauty in imperfection, transience, and incompleteness. A cracked tea bowl repaired with gold. A garden that has clearly been touched by weather. A poem that trails off before its expected resolution. These aren't failures of craft. They're acknowledgments of something true about the nature of existence — that everything is in process, that arrival is an illusion, that the crack in the bowl is where the light enters, as someone once beautifully put it.

Or consider the unfinished works that have haunted us most powerfully across history. Schubert's Unfinished Symphony, with its two complete movements and its abrupt silence where a third and fourth should have been. Kafka's novels, abandoned mid-sentence, edited against his dying wishes into something that wasn't supposed to survive. Michelangelo's *Prisoners*, those figures emerging from marble but never fully released from it, arms and torsos straining toward a completion that never came. There is something in these works that finished things often can't achieve — a kind of tension, a vibrating possibility, a sense that something is being reached for.

The *Prisoners* especially stop me every time I encounter them. Michelangelo himself said something about how the sculptor's job is to remove the marble that isn't the figure, to liberate what's already there. But these figures were never liberated. They remain caught between existence and non-existence, between stone and air. And somehow this makes them more alive, not less. More human. Because isn't that precisely where most of us live — partially emerged, partially still stone, reaching?

I think there is genuine comfort available to us in this reframing, and not the cheap comfort of excuse-making. I'm not suggesting we celebrate procrastination or use incompleteness as a shield against the hard work of finishing things. Some things genuinely need to be finished. Reports and promises and bridges, for instance. There are practical completions that matter enormously.

But the inner life operates by different rules. The half-read book left on a nightstand is not a defeat. It is a conversation paused. The language you started learning and set aside has already changed something in you — given you a new angle on your own tongue, loosened some assumption you didn't know you were carrying. You absorbed more than you realize. Incompletion is not the same as failure to begin, and it is not the same as wasted time.

What if we thought about our lives less like novels, which have a structure that moves toward resolution, and more like rivers, which are never the same twice and don't resolve so much as continue? A river doesn't fail because it hasn't reached the sea yet. It is simply being a river, right now, in this particular bend and current. The meaning isn't located at the mouth. It's distributed across every inch of moving water.

I finished a book recently — actually finished it, reached the last page, closed it — and felt an almost physical sadness. Not because the book was sad, though it was, but because it was over. The world I'd been inhabiting was sealed off. Those characters had stopped becoming and started simply being what they were. Completion had frozen them. In the days before I finished it, while I still had a hundred pages left, there was this quality of aliveness to the experience, a sense of unresolved possibility. Every time I picked it up I was entering a space where things could still turn out differently.

This is the strange energy of the unfinished thing. It stays warm. It keeps its options open. It remains in dialogue with you.

I have a novel I've been not-writing for years. Or rather, I've been writing it intermittently, in bursts and then retreats, accumulating scenes that don't yet know how to find each other. I used to be ashamed of this. I'd tell people "I'm working on a novel" with a slightly defensive inflection, as if I expected them to say *yes, but when will you be done?* Now I'm less sure that being done is the right north star. The writing of it has taught me things. The not-finishing of it has kept a certain part of my mind engaged and open and asking questions. If I finished it — really finished it, nailed the last page — something would close.

Maybe that's worth it. Maybe it would be worth it. But I'm less convinced than I used to be that the finished artifact is straightforwardly more valuable than the living process that produces it.

We are all, in the end, unfinished things. Working drafts of people, carrying abandoned projects and suspended intentions and half-formed thoughts we keep meaning to return to. The drawer that won't close.

I've stopped wanting to close it. I've started finding something almost tender in its overflow — evidence not of failure but of appetite, of a life capacious enough to accumulate more beginnings than any one person could possibly see through to the end.

The crack in the bowl. The prisoners reaching. The symphony that stops.

Still something. Still resonant. Still reaching.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
# The Strange Dignity of Waiting

There is a bench in a train station I visited once, years ago, that I have never stopped thinking about. It was wooden, painted green so many times that the paint had become its own kind of substance — layered and geological, chipping at the edges to reveal earlier greens beneath, lighter shades from decades past, the ghost of someone's optimistic renovation. The bench was bolted to a concrete floor that had been worn smooth by the passage of shoes, and on it sat a man reading a newspaper with the absolute focused calm of someone who had nowhere else to be.

I watched him for longer than was probably polite.

What struck me was not the man himself but the quality of his stillness. He was not waiting impatiently. He was not checking a phone or sighing or performing the modern ritual anxiety that most of us bring to waiting — that restless demonstration that we have important things to do elsewhere, that our time is valuable, that waiting is an insult to our productivity. He was simply sitting. The train would come or it would not. Time was passing, and he was letting it pass, and this seemed to suit him fine.

I think about waiting a lot. Not the frustrated kind, but the other kind — the dignified, almost philosophical variety that seems to have become increasingly rare. Waiting used to be something humans were quite good at. Farmers waited for rain, for seasons, for harvests that might or might not arrive. Sailors waited for wind. Lovers sent letters and waited weeks for replies, and in that interval of not-knowing, something happened — some interior activity, some ripening of feeling, some imaginative work that instant communication has made largely obsolete. You had to sit with the uncertainty. You had to furnish the silence yourself.

Now we have engineered waiting almost entirely out of existence, and I wonder sometimes what we have lost in doing so.

The buffering wheel on a video that takes four seconds to load produces in modern people a spike of genuine frustration. Studies have documented this. We have recalibrated our tolerance so precisely to the speed of our technologies that even tiny interruptions feel like violations. We have, in some sense, forgotten how to be in time. We have forgotten that time is not simply a medium through which we move toward goals, a transparent fluid in which events are suspended — but is itself an experience, and waiting is one of its most concentrated forms.

The great religions understood this. Contemplative traditions across cultures have taken the practice of sitting still and doing nothing, of waiting without object, and elevated it into something sacred. The mystic waiting for God. The Zen student waiting for nothing in particular, which turns out to be waiting for everything. There is a reason these traditions converged on stillness and patience as virtues rather than coincidences of temperament. They had noticed something about the structure of attention — that it deepens only under certain conditions, that understanding arrives on its own schedule, that you cannot rush your way to clarity.

But I do not want to be overly romantic about any of this. Waiting has also been a form of oppression. Whole groups of people have had waiting imposed on them — have been made to wait in lines, in anterooms, in inferior positions — as a demonstration of power. The colonized wait. The poor wait in government offices while the wealthy do not. Waiting rooms are not neutral spaces; they encode social hierarchies with perfect efficiency. To make someone wait is to assert that your time matters more than theirs, and this has been wielded as a weapon for as long as there have been waiting rooms to wield it in.

So there are two kinds of waiting, I think, and they are almost opposites despite sharing a name. There is the waiting that is done to you, that is imposed as a tax on your lesser status, that grinds you down. And there is the waiting that you choose — or that you make your peace with — which can become something closer to attention, or presence, or the particular patience that allows difficult things to become clear.

The man on the green bench had achieved the second kind. Or perhaps he had simply lived long enough to know the difference, and had decided that a train station on an ordinary Tuesday was not worth the expenditure of agitation.

I have been trying, with mixed results, to learn this. To let there be gaps in the day without filling them. To not reach for the phone every time a conversation pauses, every time I am standing in a line, every time the elevator is slower than it should be.
